generatedBy: property-testing-phase0-audit workflow (26 agents: 11 audit + 11 adversarial verify + 4 synthesis)
method: Read-only fan-out audit across 11 bounded-context lanes with per-finding adversarial verification against the live code + Prisma @@unique/@@index constraints. False positives (already guarded by a unique constraint, $transaction, or idempotency row) were dismissed.

severityModel.Critical: money/bookings/inventory/subscriptions/auth/permissions/irreversible state can be corrupted under concurrency
severityModel.High: read-check-write without transaction/constraint/idempotency; or duplicate webhook can duplicate side effects
severityModel.Medium: concurrent ops may cause stale reads or lost updates but impact is recoverable
severityModel.Low: pure domain logic lacks property coverage but no concurrency risk identified

laneSummaries.title: Booking / Scheduling — double-booking, slot confirm at-most-once
laneSummaries.summary: The booking-scheduling lane has one Critical double-confirm race on the bare read-check-write `confirm` endpoint, one Critical slot-booking race in `ScheduleAppointmentUseCase` where the conflict-check/save sequence is not wrapped in a single serialisable transaction, one High lost-update risk on reschedule (load-mutate-save outside a transaction), and one High risk on `batchUpdateStatus` which skips status pre-conditions entirely. The Prisma `Appointment` model carries no `@@unique` constraint on `(organizerId, startTime, endTime)` or `(tenantId, organizerId, startTime, endTime)`, so the DB cannot backstop any of the first three. `AppointmentAttendee` and `AppointmentCase` DO have `@@unique` constraints that fully protect addAttendee/linkToCase races, so those are not real findings. Zero property-based tests exist anywhere in the repo (fast-check is only a transitive dependency; `test.prop` / `fc.assert` are entirely absent). All existing tests use mocked Prisma clients, so no real-DB concurrency is verified today.

laneSummaries.title: Routing / Assignment — read-check-write lost update, double assignment
laneSummaries.summary: The routing-assignment lane has one genuine Critical race and two High races. The Critical race is in ticket-routing.router.ts: all routing decisions (SLA check, rule match, agent candidacy) are made outside the routeTicket transaction using stale reads, allowing two concurrent autoRoute calls on the same ticket to independently select the same agent and both commit — resulting in duplicate capacity increments on the agent and duplicate RoutingAudit rows. The first High race is the capacity overrun: both LeadRoutingService and TicketRoutingService read agentAvailability for capacity-filtering inside the transaction but there is no conditional guard on the updateMany (e.g. WHERE currentCapacity < maxCapacity), so concurrent transactions that each see an under-capacity agent can both increment, pushing currentCapacity past maxCapacity. AgentAvailability has no CHECK constraint or @@unique on capacity. The second High race is in bulk reassign across all three routers (account, contact, opportunity): each item's existing-owner is read outside the per-item transaction (TOCTOU), meaning a concurrent reassign can flip the owner between the outer findFirst and the inner updateMany; the code correctly handles this with updateMany returning count=0, but the audit log fired before the transaction captures the stale previousOwnerId. Two Medium races also exist: the routing.reorder mutation does a count-then-transaction check-then-act that allows a cross-tenant rule injection; and the forceReroute path in LeadRoutingService races with a concurrent ordinary routeLead on the same lead. Zero concurrency tests (scheduler, model-based, or real-db) exist anywhere in the codebase for this lane.

laneSummaries.title: Duplicate detection & Merge — concurrent create dup, lost merge
laneSummaries.summary: The dedupe-merge lane has strong transactional guards for the core merge path (PrismaContactRepository.mergeInTransaction wraps all child re-parenting in a single $transaction with an in-transaction tenant re-verify) and the linkContactsByDomain fallback path also uses $transaction. The duplicate-rule evaluator is a pure function with zero concurrency surface. However, five distinct concurrency risks remain. The most severe is the contact/lead create path: ContactService.createContact and LeadService.createLead both execute an existsByEmail check and then a save as two separate, non-transactional operations — under concurrent requests with identical email + tenantId the @@unique constraint is the only backstop, but the service layer returns a generic PersistenceError that the inbound router does not reliably map to an idempotent response. The second critical risk is the concurrent anti-parallel merge (A->B while B->A): both calls will each pass the pre-merge findById reads (both rows exist at that moment), and the first merge to commit will delete one row; the second will then hit CrossTenantOrNotFoundError inside the transaction and roll back cleanly — but only because the repository re-verifies inside the tx. This is already partially mitigated at the adapter level. The inbound router's tag-based idempotency check (findFirst then createLead) is a classic check-then-act race with no transaction or unique constraint on submissionId. The PrismaContactRepository.existsByEmail and findByEmail methods query by email alone (no tenantId filter), creating a cross-tenant false-positive that blocks creation of the same email in a second tenant. Account creation has no name/website uniqueness constraint at the DB level; duplicate-detection is advisory only, meaning concurrent creates of the same account name bypass both the check and the DB entirely.

laneSummaries.title: Webhooks / Outbox — duplicate event effects, exactly-once dispatch
laneSummaries.summary: The outbox dispatch path has a genuine but bounded concurrency posture. The strongest guard is in `PrismaOutboxRepository.fetchPendingEvents`, which issues a raw `SELECT … FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` query (line 137–172), correctly preventing concurrent pollers from claiming the same row. However, three real races remain un-mitigated. First, `OutboxEventBusAdapter.publish` performs a `findFirst`-then-`create` check for duplicate events (lines 66–102) outside any transaction, with no `@@unique` index on `idempotency_key` in the `DomainEvent` model — concurrent callers can both pass the check and write duplicate rows. Second, `OutboxPoller.processEvent` dispatches the event and then calls `markAsPublished` as two separate non-atomic operations (lines 203–206 of pollOutbox.ts); a crash between them leaves the row in PENDING status and the event is re-dispatched on the next poll cycle, causing duplicate handler side-effects despite the DB lock. Third, `WebhookFramework`'s `IdempotencyStore` is a pure in-memory `Map` with a check-then-set pattern (lines 612 and 659 of framework.ts) that is not safe under concurrent async requests in a single Node.js event loop — two `await handle()` calls with the same event ID can both pass the `has()` check before either reaches the `set()` call. The `publishAll` path is correctly wrapped in a `withTransaction` call, but that transaction uses the module-level singleton `prisma` client rather than the injected `this.prisma`, creating a correctness gap in test and multi-tenant scenarios. Zero property tests using fast-check exist anywhere in the repository.

laneSummaries.title: Queue / Background jobs — at-most-once business effect, retry idempotency
laneSummaries.summary: The workers-queue-jobs lane has a solid foundation in several critical areas: BullMQ provides native at-most-once delivery via Redis-backed job locking; the outbox poller uses FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED (PrismaOutboxRepository.ts:171) which correctly prevents two workers picking the same event; and all three AI insight upserts (scoring, prediction, insight-generation) land on Prisma upsert backed by @@unique([leadId, tenantId]) (schema line 2707) and @unique on contactId (schema line 2717), so the DB itself enforces the idempotency invariant on retries. The primary risk concentration is in the MaintenanceScheduler (scheduled-jobs.ts): five setInterval jobs each use a findFirst-then-create pattern for deduplication with no transaction or unique constraint to guard the gap, making duplicate notifications the dominant failure mode when the worker is horizontally scaled or when a slow interval fires before the prior one completes. A secondary structural defect is that enqueueAIScoring calls canRetry() on the global RetryBudgetTracker singleton but never calls consumeRetry(), making the budget enforcement permanently ineffective (budget never drains). The AIInsight duplicate-check in insight-generation.job.ts (line 742-750) uses the same findFirst-then-create gap without a unique constraint on (tenantId, title, entityId), so two concurrent insight jobs for the same tenant can both pass the check and insert duplicate rows. Zero property-based tests exist in this lane; the existing tests are all mock-based unit tests with no concurrency dimension.

laneSummaries.title: RBAC / Auth / Session / MFA — permission derivation, concurrent role mutation
laneSummaries.summary: The RBAC/auth/session lane has moderate-to-critical concurrency posture issues centred on three axes. First, both SessionService and MfaService use module-level JavaScript Map stores (sessionsByUser, sessionStore, challengeStore, mfaSettingsCache) that are shared across all concurrent requests in a single process; read-check-write sequences on these maps are not protected by any lock or CAS, making them vulnerable to lost-update and count-bypass races under async interleaving. Second, RBACService.assignRole and setUserPermission each perform a two-step findUnique-then-upsert without wrapping the pair in a $transaction, creating a small but real TOCTOU window where a concurrent revocation can interleave between the lookup and the write; the @@unique([userId, roleId]) and @@unique([userId, permissionId]) constraints in the Prisma schema do prevent duplicate rows from being silently inserted, but they do not prevent the revoke-then-re-assign race that leaves a user with elevated permissions briefly. Third, the in-process permission cache (1-minute TTL, Map keyed by userId:role) is shared across all instances that share the same module singleton, and cache invalidation (clearCache) is local-only, meaning a concurrent assign on one connection clears the cache on that instance while another concurrent read on the same singleton still returns the stale cached permission set for up to 60 seconds. No property tests or scheduler-race tests exist anywhere in this lane; all tests are unit-mocked only.

laneSummaries.title: Quota / Budget / Capacity — non-atomic decrement below zero
laneSummaries.summary: The quota/budget/capacity lane has three meaningful concurrency risks. The most critical is in ZepMemoryAdapter: episode counting is maintained as in-process integer state and written back to the DB as an absolute overwrite (not an atomic increment), so two concurrent adapter instances for the same tenant can silently under-count or over-count, allowing the 1,000-episode free-tier limit to be exceeded. Second, both LeadRoutingService and TicketRoutingService select eligible agents by reading currentCapacity outside the routing transaction, then increment unconditionally inside the transaction with no WHERE currentCapacity < maxCapacity guard and no DB CHECK constraint — concurrent routing bursts assign the same over-capacity agent to multiple leads/tickets simultaneously. Third, the zep-budget reset mutation does a bare findUnique then upsert without wrapping both in a single transaction, allowing two concurrent resets to produce an inconsistent audit trail. The query-budget detector itself (ADR-053) is correctly scoped to AsyncLocalStorage per-request stores, is single-threaded by Node.js semantics, and has an existing interleaving isolation test; no race is present there.

laneSummaries.title: Entitlement / Tenant Modules — subscription-like state transitions
laneSummaries.summary: The entitlement/tenant-module lane has no `$transaction` anywhere in `PrismaTenantModuleRepository`. The `@@unique([tenantId, moduleId])` constraint on `TenantModule` prevents duplicate rows and makes individual `upsert` calls idempotent, which mitigates the most obvious duplicate-row race. However, five real concurrency exposures remain. First, `syncModulesToPlan` fans out upserts via `Promise.all` without a wrapping transaction and then calls `getEnabledModules` outside that fan-out, so a concurrent toggle can produce a torn final list. Second, `getEnabledModules` issues two sequential non-transactional reads (plan tier then overrides), which can return a merged result that never actually existed as a committed state. Third, `handleSubscriptionWebhook` is a `publicProcedure` with no Stripe-signature verification and no idempotency table for processed event IDs, so Stripe retries or replays trigger concurrent `syncModulesToPlan` calls. Fourth, `ensureCustomer` has a classic read-check-write on `user.stripeCustomerId` that can create duplicate Stripe customers under double-submit. Fifth, `updateSubscription` syncs modules to the plan read from the DB after Stripe confirms, but the `Workspace.plan` column is never written in this path, so the sync always uses the pre-change plan tier. Additionally, the process-level `billingCache` Map is not coherent across replicas, leaving post-mutation stale reads in multi-instance deployments. No property tests exist for any of these invariants; `@fast-check/vitest` is absent and no `fc.assert` call appears anywhere in the codebase.

laneSummaries.title: Audit log — exactly-once recording of critical state changes
laneSummaries.summary: The audit-log lane has five substantive concurrency risks. The most severe is an in-memory shared-mutable hash-chain pointer (`this.previousHash`) on `DurableAuditLogAdapter` that is read before the transaction commits and written after, meaning any two concurrent calls to `logSecurityEvent` or `logBatchEvents` on the same adapter instance will produce hash chains that are silently broken — every event will carry the same stale `previousHash` value and the chain will become unverifiable without any error being raised. The second critical risk is `logLoginSuccess` / `logLoginFailure` in `AuditLogger`, which sequentially awaits two independent DB writes (one to `AuditLogEntry`, one to `SecurityEvent`) with no wrapping transaction; a failure between the two yields an auth event that appears in one table but not the other, making compliance reconstruction incorrect. Third, the `DurableAuditLogAdapter` is configured against a custom `AuditPrismaClient` interface whose `securityEvent.findUnique({ where: { eventId } })` has no counterpart in the production Prisma schema (`SecurityEvent` has no `eventId` field — it uses `id`), so `verifyLogIntegrity` will silently always return `EVENT_NOT_FOUND` in production. Fourth, the `AuditLogger` singleton (`getAuditLogger` in `audit/index.ts`) captures the first `PrismaClient` passed to it, but multiple request contexts (each with their own connection pool and tenant middleware) all resolve to the same instance, meaning tenant-scoped connection extensions are bypassed for all audit writes after the first call. Fifth, the async buffer `flush()` method does a non-atomic `splice` + `Promise.all` + `unshift` pattern: if two concurrent flushes race (one from the `batchSize` threshold path and one from the timer), the same entries can be double-written. All five issues have zero real-DB integration test coverage; the existing test suite exclusively uses mocked Prisma clients.

laneSummaries.title: Pure domain invariants — value objects, status machines, normalization, round-trips
laneSummaries.summary: The pure-domain layer of IntelliFlow CRM is well-structured around immutable value objects and aggregate roots using the Result monad pattern. No infrastructure dependencies exist. Zero property-based tests exist anywhere in the repo — fast-check is only a transitive dependency of vitest and cannot be used directly; any property tests must be written using vitest with manual shrinking or a separately-installed fc package. The most significant gaps are: (1) Money arithmetic has an undocumented duplicate-currency entry in SUPPORTED_CURRENCIES ('GBP' appears twice), a missing 'USD' entry, and no property tests covering commutativity/associativity across the boundary cases; (2) Invoice.recalculateTotals silently swallows add/subtract failures via if-isSuccess guards, so a failed partial update leaves totals in a stale/inconsistent state with no observable error; (3) module-level counters (invoiceSequence, receiptSequence, ticketCounter) are process-global mutable singletons that produce non-deterministic, colliding numbers under parallel test workers; (4) Opportunity.weightedValue routes through Money.create(decimal_amount) rather than Money.fromCents, reintroducing the float-rounding path for very large or fractional probability values; (5) several status-machine transitions (Task.complete bypasses IN_PROGRESS guard via direct field write, Ticket allows CLOSED→ARCHIVED as a single hop despite the CLOSED isTerminal guard inconsistency in changePriority) have partial unit-test coverage but no exhaustive enumeration of all invalid pairs. No concurrency risks exist at the pure-domain layer itself (all aggregates are single-threaded in-memory objects); severity ratings reflect pure-property gaps only.

laneSummaries.title: Test infrastructure inventory — what exists vs what Phase 1 must add
laneSummaries.summary: The IntelliFlow CRM monorepo has a substantial Vitest 4.x infrastructure (30k+ tests, 16+ projects, Istanbul coverage) but has zero property-based testing capability: fast-check is entirely absent as a direct dependency (@fast-check/vitest is not installed; fast-check@3.23.2 exists only transitively via vitest's pure-rand), and no test.prop or fc.assert calls exist anywhere in the codebase. The integration test harness (tests/integration/setup.ts) is functional — it supports testcontainers/pgvector graceful-skip, real Prisma migrations, and beforeEach TRUNCATE — but exposes a single shared PrismaClient singleton via getTestPrismaClient(), which prevents genuine concurrent-connection race tests. A DatabaseTestHelper class in tests/utils/db.ts is almost entirely TODO stubs with no real Prisma wiring. Test fixtures provide only canonical UUID constants (packages/test-fixtures/src/ids.ts) with no domain builders or arbitraries. CI tiers currently map as: PR = unit+coverage+build-preview (pr-checks.yml), push/PR to main/develop = full unit+integration+build (ci.yml), nightly at 03:00 UTC = system audit tiers 1+2+3 (system-audit-nightly.yml). There is no smoke/standard/stress property tier, no test:property npm script, no concurrent-request helper, and no deterministic seed/replay infrastructure. The critical gaps Phase 1 must fill are: install fast-check + @fast-check/vitest as direct dev dependencies, create the tests/property/ support tree (arbitraries, commands, model, scheduler, database, assertions), add a multi-client DB sandbox helper, add concurrent-request helpers, add npm scripts test:property[:smoke|:stress] + test:concurrency, wire smoke into PR CI and stress into the nightly job, and document deterministic seed replay for CI failure diagnosis.

findings.title: Hash-chain previousHash mutated after transaction commit — concurrent calls produce broken chains
findings.businessRule: Every audit entry must chain to the preceding entry via previousHash so tampering is detectable; concurrent writes must not silently produce duplicate previousHash values.
invalidStates: Two concurrent logSecurityEvent calls both write previousHash = 'GENESIS' instead of one being the hash of the other
verifyLogIntegrity returns valid=false for all events after the first concurrent pair, silently destroying the compliance guarantee
logBatchEvents loop writes events 2..N with the same previousHash as event 1 because this.previousHash is only updated after the outer transaction commits

evidence.note: `private previousHash: string = 'GENESIS'` — single shared mutable field on the class instance.
`previousHash: this.previousHash` is read synchronously before the `$transaction` call starts.
`this.previousHash = integrityHash` is written AFTER the `await this.prisma.$transaction` resolves — two concurrent calls both read the old value before either write completes.

Same pattern repeated inside `logBatchEvents`: previousHash read at line 191, updated at 218 inside the loop, but the entire transaction wraps multiple events — events 2..N all read the pre-transaction hash.
'Concurrent safety' describe block only runs `Promise.all(events.map(...))` against a mocked `$transaction` that completes instantly — not a real concurrency test.

findings.existingMitigations: none found — no DB-level sequence constraint on previousHash; no optimistic lock or mutex around the read-modify-write cycle
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add a per-tenant sequence column or use a SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the last hash row to serialize chain extension; alternatively store previousHash exclusively in DB and fetch it inside the transaction.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.tuple(fc.array(fc.record({ eventType: fc.string(), tenantId: fc.constant('t1') }), { minLength: 2, maxLength: 10 }), fc.scheduler())
commandSequenceCandidates: concurrentLogSecurityEvent: [logSecurityEvent(e1), logSecurityEvent(e2)] in parallel — assert previousHashes form a strict chain
interleavedBatch: logBatchEvents([e1,e2]) concurrent with logSecurityEvent(e3) — assert no duplicate previousHash in DB
fixStrategyCandidates: Move previousHash retrieval inside the $transaction callback by fetching the latest row with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE, eliminating the in-memory mutable field entirely
Add an explicit async mutex (p-limit(1) per tenant) around the read-hash / write-entry cycle
Replace previousHash with a DB-sequence-backed counter stored atomically in the same transaction
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. `this.previousHash` is read at DurableAuditLogAdapter.ts:110 before the `$transaction` call starts, and written at :148 after it resolves. Two concurrent `logSecurityEvent` calls will both read the current value (e.g. 'GENESIS') before either write commits, producing two rows with identical `previousHash`, silently breaking the tamper-detection chain. No DB-level sequence, mutex, or SELECT FOR UPDATE guards this. The 'Concurrent safety' test at :421-493 runs against a synchronous mock so it never observes the race. Same issue exists in `logBatchEvents` at :191/:218.

findings.title: Double-confirm: bare read-check-write on appointment status without transaction or conditional update
findings.businessRule: An appointment slot cannot be confirmed twice; confirm is only valid when the current status is SCHEDULED.
invalidStates: status=CONFIRMED written twice by two concurrent requests, both reading status=SCHEDULED before either commits
AppointmentConfirmedEvent emitted twice, triggering duplicate ICS sends and duplicate reminder scheduling

evidence.note: confirm: findUnique reads status, checks === 'SCHEDULED', then a separate update sets CONFIRMED. No $transaction wraps these two operations. Two concurrent requests can both observe SCHEDULED and both write CONFIRMED.
Appointment model has no @@unique on (organizerId, startTime, endTime) or status-transition guard. No DB-level constraint prevents status from being written twice.

withTransaction uses default $transaction isolation (PostgreSQL READ COMMITTED). The confirm endpoint does not use withTransaction at all — the two Prisma calls are on the raw ctx.prismaWithTenant client.

findings.existingMitigations: none found
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Atomic conditional update: update WHERE status='SCHEDULED' and check rows-affected=1; OR wrap findUnique+update in a $transaction with RepeatableRead/Serializable isolation; OR add a DB partial unique index that disallows a second CONFIRMED row for the same appointment.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ appointmentId: fc.uuidV4(), concurrentClients: fc.constant(2) }) — two scheduler tasks racing to confirm the same appointment ID
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: findUnique(id) → reads SCHEDULED
T2: findUnique(id) → reads SCHEDULED
T1: update(id, {status: CONFIRMED}) → writes CONFIRMED
T2: update(id, {status: CONFIRMED}) → writes CONFIRMED (second confirm wins)
fixStrategyCandidates: Replace findUnique+update with a single atomic Prisma update that includes { where: { id, status: 'SCHEDULED' } } and throws NOT_FOUND if rows_affected=0
Wrap both Prisma calls in withTransaction({ isolationLevel: 'RepeatableRead' }) so T2 waits for T1's commit and then sees CONFIRMED, failing its own status check

Add a DB-level partial unique index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON appointments(id) WHERE status='CONFIRMED' — prevents inserting a second CONFIRMED row (not directly expressible in Prisma schema but achievable via a raw migration)
findings.verificationReason: apps/api/src/modules/legal/appointments.router.ts lines 720-748: bare findUnique (line 720) then update (line 738) with no $transaction wrapper and no conditional WHERE status='SCHEDULED' on the update call. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma lines 1602-1657: Appointment model has no @@unique or CHECK constraint that prevents writing CONFIRMED twice. packages/db/src/client.ts lines 173-179: withTransaction is not used at all in the confirm handler. Two concurrent requests both reading status=SCHEDULED before either commits will both succeed and write CONFIRMED. The router also bypasses Appointment.confirm() in the domain entity (packages/domain/src/legal/appointments/Appointment.ts lines 393-414), so no AppointmentConfirmedEvent is emitted from this code path — the duplicate-event concern is moot here, but the double-write of CONFIRMED status against the invariant 'confirm is only valid when current status is SCHEDULED' is a genuine unguarded race.

findings.title: Slot double-booking: conflict-check and slot creation are not in a single serialisable transaction
findings.businessRule: Overlapping appointments for the same organiser/resource cannot both be confirmed — the conflict gate must be evaluated and the booking persisted atomically.
invalidStates: Two appointments with identical (organizerId, startTime, endTime) both reach SCHEDULED status
Two overlapping appointments (partial overlap) both confirmed for the same attendee, violating the no-overlap invariant

evidence.note: fetchConflictCandidates (a plain SELECT) runs at line 107, ConflictDetector.checkConflicts at line 108, then appointmentRepository.save at line 115. No transaction or advisory lock spans lines 107-115. Two concurrent requests for overlapping slots can both observe zero conflicts before either persists.

save() runs inside withTransaction (line 494) which wraps the upsert + attendee + case sync. But the transaction does NOT include the prior findForConflictCheck SELECT; it only serialises the write side.

The Appointment model has no @@unique on (tenantId, organizerId, startTime, endTime) or any overlap constraint. PostgreSQL has no built-in OVERLAPS constraint expressible in Prisma schema — a DB-side exclusion constraint on the tsrange is needed.
findOverlapping is a plain SELECT with no FOR SHARE/FOR UPDATE hint. Any READ COMMITTED transaction cannot prevent phantom inserts of overlapping rows between the SELECT and the subsequent INSERT.

findings.existingMitigations: none found
findings.requiredDbConstraints: PostgreSQL EXCLUSION CONSTRAINT on (tenantId, tsrange(startTime, endTime)) using the && (overlaps) operator, or at minimum @@unique([tenantId, organizerId, startTime]) to block identical-start duplicates; wrap SELECT+INSERT in a SERIALIZABLE transaction so T2's phantom-read causes a serialisation failure.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ organizerId: fc.uuidV4(), startTime: fc.date({ min: new Date('2025-01-01') }), durationMinutes: fc.integer({ min: 15, max: 120 }) }) — generate two overlapping slots for same organiser, fire concurrently
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: findForConflictCheck([orgId], {start, end}) → returns []
T2: findForConflictCheck([orgId], {start, end}) → returns []
T1: save(appointment1) → INSERT rows
T2: save(appointment2) → INSERT rows (duplicate slot)
fixStrategyCandidates: Add a PostgreSQL EXCLUSION CONSTRAINT (raw migration) on (organizerId, tsrange(startTime, endTime, '[]')) WITH (gist) so the DB rejects overlapping inserts with a constraint violation that the use case catches and converts to a ConflictDetectionError

Wrap the entire execute() body in withTransactionOptions({ isolationLevel: 'Serializable' }) so any phantom-read of a concurrently inserted overlapping appointment triggers a serialisation failure and automatic retry
Introduce an advisory lock keyed by (tenantId + organizerId) that is acquired before the conflict check and released after save, serialising all booking attempts per organiser
findings.verificationReason: packages/application/src/usecases/scheduling/ScheduleAppointment.ts lines 107-115: fetchConflictCandidates (line 107) is a plain SELECT outside any transaction; appointmentRepository.save (line 115) wraps only the write side in withTransaction (packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaAppointmentRepository.ts lines 494-531). packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaAppointmentRepository.ts lines 229-244: findOverlapping SELECT has no FOR SHARE/FOR UPDATE hint and runs under READ COMMITTED. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma lines 1602-1657: no @@unique([tenantId, organizerId, startTime, endTime]) and no exclusion constraint on the overlapping time range. Under READ COMMITTED, two concurrent ScheduleAppointmentUseCase executions for overlapping slots both observe zero conflicts before either INSERT commits, producing two saved overlapping appointments.

findings.title: handleSubscriptionWebhook has no signature verification and no idempotency key — replay triggers concurrent module syncs
findings.businessRule: A Stripe subscription webhook event must be processed exactly once; replays or retries must not trigger additional module-state mutations.
invalidStates: Two concurrent webhook retries both enter syncModulesToPlan, one reads partially-applied state from the other and returns an incomplete module list to the caller
A spoofed webhook with a downgrade plan tier disables modules the tenant paid for

evidence.note: Comment explicitly states 'In production, this would verify the Stripe signature' — verification is not implemented.
Procedure is publicProcedure — no auth, no rate limit, no signature check gate.
syncModulesToPlan called without any idempotency check. Stripe retries deliver the same event ID; each retry triggers a full sync.
No processed_stripe_events or webhook_idempotency table exists. DomainEvent table (line 1560) has no stripeEventId column.
updateSubscription also calls syncModulesToPlan independently, creating a second concurrent trigger path when both fire near-simultaneously.

findings.existingMitigations: @@unique([tenantId, moduleId]) makes each upsert idempotent in isolation. However the torn-read race (RACE-ENTIT-01, RACE-ENTIT-02) is amplified with concurrent invocations.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add a processed_stripe_events table with (event_id STRING @unique) and check before every sync. Implement stripe.webhooks.constructEvent() signature verification.

tRPC endpoint processes, starts syncModulesToPlan
Stripe retry fires same event before first sync completes

fixStrategyCandidates: Verify Stripe-Signature header via stripe.webhooks.constructEvent() before any processing

findings.verificationReason: Confirmed at apps/api/src/modules/billing/billing.router.ts:1469-1534. The handleSubscriptionWebhook procedure is publicProcedure (line 1473) with no authentication, no rate limiting, and the explicit comment at line 1469 'In production, this would verify the Stripe signature' confirms verification is not implemented. There is no processed_stripe_events table in schema.prisma (verified: DomainEvent at lines 1560-1587 has no stripeEventId column, and a full-schema grep for processed_stripe/stripe_event/webhook_idempotency returns no results). The @@unique([tenantId, moduleId]) guard at schema.prisma:302 makes each individual upsert idempotent (correct), but it does not prevent two concurrent syncModulesToPlan invocations from interleaving their upserts and producing a torn read via the trailing getEnabledModules() (see RACE-ENTIT-01). Crucially, the signature-absent + idempotency-absent combination means any unauthenticated actor can POST a spoofed downgrade event and strip module access for any tenant. The separate general-purpose webhook handler at apps/api/src/webhooks/handler.ts:317 has signature verification but handleSubscriptionWebhook does NOT route through it.

findings.title: Consumed backup code never persisted to DB — same code reusable after cache eviction or process restart
findings.businessRule: A backup code must be invalidated exactly once upon successful use; it must not be accepted on any subsequent attempt.
invalidStates: User authenticates with backup code X; code X is removed from the in-memory object but DB still has code X; after process restart (or cache miss), code X is re-accepted
On a different Railway instance that never had the cache entry, code X is always accepted

evidence.note: validateChallengeCode for method='backup' mutates userMfaSettings.backupCodes = result.updatedCodes (line 470) in-memory on the caller's object, removing the used code from the local reference.
verifyChallenge returns { success: true } after challengeStore.delete(challengeId) with no call to saveUserMfaSettings. The consumed backup code is never written back to DB.

The production auth flow calls mfaService.verifyChallenge(input.challengeId, input.code) with no userMfaSettings argument, so backup code verification always returns false in practice — but any caller that does pass userMfaSettings (tests, future endpoints) would silently accept the same backup code on any subsequent login after cache eviction or process restart.
Test verifies userSettings.backupCodes has length 7 in-memory after use, but never asserts saveUserMfaSettings was called. DB persistence gap is untested.

findings.existingMitigations: none found — no saveUserMfaSettings call in verifyChallenge; no DB write path for consumed backup codes.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: No new DB constraint needed. Fix: call await saveUserMfaSettings({ ...userMfaSettings }) inside verifyChallenge after validateChallengeCode returns valid for method='backup'.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.hexaString({ minLength: 10, maxLength: 10 }), { minLength: 8, maxLength: 8 }) for backup code set; use same code index twice across two mfaService instances with shared DB
commandSequenceCandidates: verifyChallenge(id, backupCode[0], settings) → success; clearCache(); getUserMfaSettings(userId) → DB fetch; verifyChallenge(id2, backupCode[0], freshSettings) → must fail but succeeds
fixStrategyCandidates: In verifyChallenge, after validateChallengeCode succeeds for method='backup', call await this.saveUserMfaSettings(userMfaSettings) before returning { success: true }
Move backup code removal to a separate DB UPDATE: UPDATE user_mfa_settings SET backup_codes = array_remove(backup_codes, $hash) WHERE user_id = $1
Use an atomic DB operation: UPDATE user_mfa_settings SET backup_codes = array_remove(backup_codes, $hash) WHERE user_id = $1 AND $hash = ANY(backup_codes) RETURNING *
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: Ticket autoRoute: all routing decisions made outside transaction — double-assignment and duplicate audit
findings.businessRule: A ticket is assigned to exactly one agent after concurrent routing; each routing call increments the selected agent's capacity exactly once.
invalidStates: ticket.assigneeId set to agent A, then immediately overwritten to agent B by second concurrent call
agentAvailability.currentCapacity incremented twice for the same ticket/agent pair
two RoutingAudit rows with same ticketId exist after a single autoRoute request pair

evidence.note: ticket.findFirst, checkSlaEscalation, findMatchingRule, and suggestAssignees are all executed OUTSIDE any transaction. These four reads determine assigneeId and capacity snapshot.
routeTicket() is called with the agent chosen from the stale reads. Two concurrent calls on the same ticketId will both reach routeTicket with the same assigneeId.

The inner $transaction only guards the ticket.update + agentAvailability.updateMany + routingAudit.create. There is no ticket-level idempotency guard: if assigneeId is already set, the transaction updates it again and creates a second audit row.
Ticket.assigneeId is nullable String — no @unique, no conditional-update constraint. Nothing prevents two concurrent writes.

findings.existingMitigations: TicketRoutingService.routeTicket rejects ARCHIVED tickets (line 151) but has no already-assigned guard. LeadRoutingService._routeLeadImpl has an idempotency guard (line 256) but TicketRoutingService does not mirror it.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add idempotency guard inside routeTicket transaction: re-read ticket.assigneeId at transaction start and throw if already assigned (unless forceReroute). Also add WHERE currentCapacity < maxCapacity to agentAvailability.updateMany.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ ticketId: fc.uuid(), tenantId: fc.uuid(), category: fc.constantFrom('BILLING','TECHNICAL','GENERAL') }) — run two concurrent autoRoute calls with identical ticketId
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: findFirst(ticket) -> T2: findFirst(ticket) -> T1: findMatchingRule -> T2: findMatchingRule -> T1: routeTicket($tx) -> T2: routeTicket($tx)
T1: suggestAssignees -> T2: suggestAssignees [same agent[0] selected] -> T1: routeTicket commits -> T2: routeTicket commits
fixStrategyCandidates: Move all routing reads (checkSlaEscalation, findMatchingRule, suggestAssignees) into the routeTicket $transaction so they execute under the same READ COMMITTED or REPEATABLE READ snapshot
Add an idempotency guard at the top of routeTicket transaction mirroring LeadRoutingService line 256
Add a SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the ticket row at transaction start to serialize concurrent routing attempts
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. In ticket-routing.router.ts:43-62 the four reads (findFirst ticket, checkSlaEscalation, findMatchingRule, suggestAssignees) all run outside any transaction. Inside routeTicket (TicketRoutingService.ts:142-193) the $transaction reads only ticket.status at line 144-153 — it does NOT read assigneeId and has NO guard against an already-assigned ticket. Two concurrent autoRoute calls for the same ticketId both pass the ARCHIVED check, both commit tx.ticket.update(assigneeId) with potentially different agents (last-write-wins), and each creates an independent routingAudit.create row. RoutingAudit schema (schema.prisma:3801-3822) has no @@unique([ticketId]), so duplicate audit rows are structurally permitted. Ticket.assigneeId (schema.prisma:2027) is a nullable String with no @unique or conditional update guard. No existing mitigation prevents the double-assignment. The LeadRoutingService idempotency guard at line 256 (lead.ownerId check inside the tx) was NOT mirrored in TicketRoutingService.

findings.title: logLoginSuccess / logLoginFailure write AuditLogEntry then SecurityEvent in two separate non-transactional awaits — partial write on error
findings.businessRule: Every authentication event must be recorded atomically in both the AuditLogEntry table and the SecurityEvent table; a crash or DB error between the two writes must not leave the logs in an inconsistent state.
invalidStates: Login success recorded in AuditLogEntry but SecurityEvent is absent — security dashboard misses the event
Login failure SecurityEvent written but AuditLogEntry absent — compliance audit trail is incomplete
Retry logic could duplicate the SecurityEvent without duplicating the AuditLogEntry, creating asymmetric counts

evidence.note: `await this.log(createLoginSuccessEntry(...))` completes first (writes to AuditLogEntry), then `await this.logSecurityEvent(createLoginSuccessSecurityEvent(...))` writes to SecurityEvent — two separate awaits, no shared $transaction.
Same pattern for logLoginFailure — if `logSecurityEvent` throws, the AuditLogEntry row already exists but the SecurityEvent row does not.
`writeEntry` does a plain `prisma.auditLogEntry.create` with no transaction.
`logSecurityEventToDb` does a plain `prisma.securityEvent.create` with no transaction — entirely independent DB write.

findings.existingMitigations: none found — both tables are independently written; no saga compensating action; no idempotency key linking the two rows
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Wrap both creates in a single prisma.$transaction; add a cross-table correlation ID column so post-hoc audits can detect mismatches.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ tenantId: fc.uuidV4(), actorId: fc.uuidV4(), ipAddress: fc.ipV4() })
commandSequenceCandidates: injectWriteErrorAfterAuditLogEntry: mock securityEvent.create to throw; assert AuditLogEntry row is rolled back or compensated
concurrentLoginEvents: fire 20 logLoginSuccess calls in parallel against a real DB; assert SecurityEvent count === AuditLogEntry count
fixStrategyCandidates: Wrap logLoginSuccess/Failure in a single $transaction that creates both rows atomically
Accept eventual consistency but add a correlation ID and a reconciliation job that detects and alerts on mismatched rows
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. `logLoginSuccess` at audit-logger.ts:137-138 issues `await this.log(createLoginSuccessEntry(...))` then `await this.logSecurityEvent(createLoginSuccessSecurityEvent(...))` as two independent awaits. `writeEntry` in writer.ts:87 does `prisma.auditLogEntry.create` and `logSecurityEventToDb` in security-handler.ts:64 does `prisma.securityEvent.create` — both are separate DB round-trips with no shared `$transaction`. A crash or DB error between the two leaves `AuditLogEntry` written but `SecurityEvent` absent (or vice versa). No saga, no correlation ID linking the two rows for post-hoc detection. Same pattern for `logLoginFailure` at :144-146.

findings.title: DurableAuditLogAdapter.verifyLogIntegrity queries by eventId which does not exist in the production SecurityEvent schema — silently always returns EVENT_NOT_FOUND
findings.businessRule: The integrity verification path must be able to retrieve a persisted SecurityEvent and recompute its hash; if the query always misses, tampered logs are undetectable.
invalidStates: verifyLogIntegrity always returns { valid: false, reason: 'EVENT_NOT_FOUND' } for all real events, making tamper detection useless
If Prisma is used directly (not the abstraction), the query will throw PrismaClientValidationError crashing the integrity check endpoint

evidence.note: `AuditPrismaClient` interface declares `securityEvent.findUnique({ where: { eventId: string } })` — the adapter's abstraction layer uses `eventId` as the lookup key.
`verifyLogIntegrity(eventId)` calls `this.prisma.securityEvent.findUnique({ where: { eventId } })` — passing `eventId` as the where clause.

Production `SecurityEvent` model has fields: `id` (@id @default(cuid())), `eventType`, `severity`, etc. There is NO `eventId` field and no `@unique` constraint on any correlation ID. `findUnique({ where: { eventId } })` will fail at runtime with a Prisma validation error or always miss.
Tests use a hand-rolled `mockPrismaClient()` whose `securityEvent.findUnique` is a plain `vi.fn()` — the schema mismatch is never caught because tests never run against the real schema.

findings.existingMitigations: none found — the AuditPrismaClient abstraction hides the schema mismatch; no integration test exercises verifyLogIntegrity against a real database
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add `eventId String @unique` to SecurityEvent model (and migration), or change verifyLogIntegrity to look up by `id` (cuid) and expose that id in AuditLogResult.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.uuidV4() representing arbitrary eventIds — property: verifyLogIntegrity(id) for any id that was just logged must return valid=true
commandSequenceCandidates: roundtrip_verify: logSecurityEvent(e) -> verifyLogIntegrity(result.eventId) -> assert valid=true
tamper_detect: logSecurityEvent(e) -> direct DB UPDATE of integrityHash -> verifyLogIntegrity -> assert valid=false
fixStrategyCandidates: Add `eventId String @unique` field to the Prisma SecurityEvent model and populate it from randomUUID() in the create call
Change `AuditLogResult.eventId` to return the Prisma-generated `id` (cuid) and update verifyLogIntegrity to use `{ where: { id: eventId } }`
Add a real-DB integration test that exercises the full log -> verify roundtrip against a testcontainer
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. The production `SecurityEvent` model in schema.prisma:1939-1980 has fields `id` (@id @default(cuid())), `eventType`, `severity`, `tenantId`, `actorId`, `actorEmail`, `actorIp`, `description`, `details`, `detected`, `detectedBy`, `blocked`, `alertSent`, `createdAt` — NO `eventId` field exists. The `AuditPrismaClient` interface at DurableAuditLogAdapter.ts:51 declares `findUnique({ where: { eventId: string } })` but this is a custom abstraction that is never validated against the real schema. `verifyLogIntegrity` at :249-252 calls `this.prisma.securityEvent.findUnique({ where: { eventId } })`. In production using the real Prisma client, this will throw a `PrismaClientValidationError` (unknown field `eventId` on SecurityEvent). Via the custom AuditPrismaClient abstraction the mock `vi.fn()` always resolves, hiding the mismatch. All tests use hand-rolled mocks (test file :21-36) — no real-DB test exercises this path. Integrity verification is completely broken against the real schema.

findings.title: OutboxEventBusAdapter.publish check-then-act on idempotency key — no DB-level unique constraint allows concurrent duplicate domain event insertion
findings.businessRule: Each domain event must be published to the outbox exactly once; two concurrent calls with the same event (e.g. retry on network timeout) must not create duplicate DomainEvent rows.
invalidStates: Two concurrent publish(event) calls for the same DomainEvent ID both pass the findFirst check and insert duplicate rows; both get dispatched to AuditEventHandler, producing doubled audit entries
Caller retries publish() after a network timeout before the first insert commits; both inserts succeed

evidence.note: `const existing = await this.prisma.domainEvent.findFirst({ where: { metadata: { path: ['idempotencyKey'], equals: idempotencyKey } } })` — then `if (existing) return` — then `await this.prisma.domainEvent.create(...)`. Classic read-check-write with no transaction.

`DomainEvent` model has no `@unique` constraint on any idempotency field. `metadata` is a plain `Json?` column with only non-unique indexes. Two concurrent `findFirst` calls both return `null`, both proceed to `create`, and both succeed — producing duplicate events.

findings.existingMitigations: none found — `existsByIdempotencyKey` is defined on `PrismaOutboxRepository` but is never called from `publish`; the inline `findFirst` in `publish` has no DB-level uniqueness backing it
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add a computed unique column or a partial unique index on a deterministic idempotency field derived from (eventType, aggregateId, aggregateType, occurredAt) outside the JSON blob; or move idempotencyKey to a top-level column with `@unique`.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ eventType: fc.constantFrom('LeadCreated','ContactUpdated'), aggregateId: fc.uuidV4(), tenantId: fc.uuidV4() })
commandSequenceCandidates: concurrent_publish: call publish(event) twice in parallel with the same event; assert domainEvent row count === 1
retry_after_timeout: publish(event) once, simulate network timeout, publish(event) again; assert no duplicate DomainEvent rows
fixStrategyCandidates: Promote idempotencyKey from metadata JSON to a top-level `String @unique` column on DomainEvent and use `createMany` with `skipDuplicates: true`
Wrap the findFirst + create in a single `$transaction` with SERIALIZABLE isolation, or use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING via `$executeRaw`
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: AuditLogEntry.eventId has no @unique constraint — retry of writeEntry silently creates duplicate audit rows
findings.businessRule: Each logical audit event must appear exactly once in the AuditLogEntry table; retrying a failed write with the same eventId must not produce a duplicate row.
invalidStates: Caller retries logLoginSuccess after a timeout; two AuditLogEntry rows exist for the same login — compliance query returns doubled event counts
async flush() at audit-logger.ts:218-228 splices entries, write fails, unshifts them back, then flushes again — each entry in toWrite is written twice (no ON CONFLICT guard)

evidence.note: `prisma.auditLogEntry.create({ data: { eventId: entry.eventId || randomUUID(), ... } })` — no upsert, no ON CONFLICT, no idempotency check before insert.

`AuditLogEntry` model: `eventId String` at line 2355 is a plain non-unique string column — no `@unique` attribute and no `@@unique` constraint. Multiple rows with the same `eventId` are freely admitted.

`logLoginSuccess` calls `await this.log(...)` then `await this.logSecurityEvent(...)`. If the caller retries the entire `logLoginSuccess` after a transient failure, both `this.log` calls create new AuditLogEntry rows. The second call will use a new `randomUUID()` at writer.ts:92, producing a duplicate with a different id but logically identical content.

findings.existingMitigations: none found — AuditLogEntry has indexes on tenantId, resourceType/resourceId/timestamp, and actorId/timestamp, but no unique constraint that would prevent duplicate eventId rows
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add `@@unique([eventId])` to `AuditLogEntry` in schema.prisma, then use `prisma.auditLogEntry.upsert` or `createMany({ skipDuplicates: true })` in writeEntry.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ tenantId: fc.uuidV4(), actorId: fc.uuidV4(), eventId: fc.uuidV4() }) — property: writeEntry called twice with same eventId produces exactly 1 row
commandSequenceCandidates: retry_write: call writeEntry(prisma, entry, config) twice with the same entry.eventId; assert auditLogEntry row count === 1
flush_retry: enqueue entry, flush fails, flush again; assert exactly one DB row per entry
fixStrategyCandidates: Add `eventId String @unique` to AuditLogEntry schema and use upsert/ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING in writeEntry
Change writeEntry to check for an existing row with the same eventId before inserting (TOCTOU — DB unique is better)

findings.title: Lost-update on reschedule: load-mutate-save outside a transaction allows cancel to be silently overwritten
findings.businessRule: A cancellation of an appointment must not be silently reversed by a concurrent reschedule that loaded the entity before the cancellation committed.
invalidStates: Appointment status reverts from CANCELLED back to SCHEDULED/CONFIRMED after the reschedule upsert wins
cancelledAt and cancellationReason fields are cleared by the reschedule upsert because the in-memory entity loaded before the cancel does not carry those fields

evidence.note: findById (line 112) reads the entity, fetchConflicts (line 132) issues another SELECT, appointment.reschedule() mutates in-memory (lines 159-164), then save() (line 171) issues an upsert. No transaction spans the load and save; a concurrent CancelAppointmentUseCase running between lines 112 and 171 commits status=CANCELLED, which is then overwritten by the reschedule's upsert.
save() uses upsert with { where: { id } }, unconditionally overwriting all columns including status. No optimistic-lock version column exists on the Appointment model.

cancel: findById then appointment.cancel() then save(). Identical load-mutate-save pattern. Two calls racing in opposite directions (cancel vs reschedule) will leave the DB in whichever state was saved last.
No version/updatedAt optimistic-lock column used in any conditional update. updatedAt is @updatedAt (server-side set by Prisma) but is not used as a precondition in upsert.

findings.existingMitigations: none found
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Optimistic concurrency: add a version (Int @default(0)) column to Appointment; update WHERE version=loadedVersion AND increment version; throw conflict if rows_affected=0. Alternatively wrap load+save in withTransactionOptions({ isolationLevel: 'RepeatableRead' }).
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ appointmentId: fc.uuidV4(), newStartTime: fc.date() }) — fire reschedule and cancel concurrently on the same appointment ID with artificial delay inserted between load and save
commandSequenceCandidates: T_reschedule: forTenant(tenantId).findById(id) → loads SCHEDULED
T_cancel: forTenant(tenantId).findById(id) → loads SCHEDULED
T_cancel: appointment.cancel() → in-memory CANCELLED
T_cancel: save(appointment) → upsert writes CANCELLED
T_reschedule: appointment.reschedule() → in-memory still SCHEDULED (stale)
T_reschedule: save(appointment) → upsert overwrites with SCHEDULED (lost cancel)
fixStrategyCandidates: Add a version column (Int @default(0)) to the Appointment Prisma model and use it as an optimistic lock: update WHERE id=id AND version=loadedVersion, increment version; throw AppointmentConflictError if zero rows updated
Wrap the entire use-case execute() in withTransactionOptions({ isolationLevel: 'RepeatableRead' }) so a concurrent cancel commit causes a serialisation failure on the reschedule's read

Before calling save(), issue a conditional update that validates status has not changed since load: prisma.appointment.update({ where: { id, status: loadedStatus }, data: {...} }) and throw if not found
findings.verificationReason: packages/application/src/usecases/scheduling/RescheduleAppointment.ts lines 110-171: findById (line 112) loads entity, fetchConflicts (line 132) issues another SELECT, appointment.reschedule() mutates in-memory (lines 159-164), then save() (line 171) issues an unconditional upsert. packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaAppointmentRepository.ts lines 496-500: upsert uses WHERE { id } only — no version/updatedAt precondition. packages/application/src/usecases/scheduling/CancelAppointment.ts lines 40-57: identical load-mutate-save pattern with the same unconstrained upsert. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma lines 1602-1610: no version column, updatedAt is @updatedAt (server-assigned, not used as a precondition). A concurrent cancel committed between load and save on the reschedule path will be silently overwritten, resurrecting a CANCELLED appointment.

findings.title: Duplicate appointment-reminder notifications: findFirst dedup + create is unguarded outside a transaction
findings.businessRule: Each appointment must generate at most one reminder notification per 1-hour window; a user must not receive duplicate reminder pushes for the same appointment.
invalidStates: User receives two (or more) identical appointment reminder notifications within the same hour when two events-worker instances process the same appointment concurrently (e.g., rolling deploy, pod restart overlap)
Notification table accumulates duplicate rows for the same (sourceId, sourceType, recipientId) tuple

evidence.note: checkAppointmentReminders: findFirst (line 493) checks for an existing notification with sourceId=appt.id and sourceType='appointment_reminder' within 1 hour. If none found, creates a new notification (line 504). No transaction spans lines 493-525 and no unique constraint on the Notification model prevents two concurrent worker instances from both observing 'alreadySent=null' and both calling create.

Notification model has no @@unique on (sourceId, sourceType) or (sourceId, sourceType, recipientId). All indexes are for read performance only. Any number of identical notification rows can be inserted concurrently.

findings.existingMitigations: none found
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add @@unique([sourceId, sourceType, recipientId]) to the Notification model to enforce idempotency at the DB level, and catch P2002 in the job to treat it as 'already sent'.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ appointmentId: fc.uuidV4(), workersCount: fc.constant(2) }) — two concurrent worker invocations firing checkAppointmentReminders for the same appointment window
commandSequenceCandidates: W1: findFirst(sourceId=X, sourceType='appointment_reminder', createdAt>=T-1h) → null
W2: findFirst(sourceId=X, sourceType='appointment_reminder', createdAt>=T-1h) → null
W1: notification.create(...) → row inserted
W2: notification.create(...) → second row inserted (duplicate reminder sent)
fixStrategyCandidates: Add @@unique([sourceId, sourceType, recipientId]) to the Notification Prisma model and use createOrUpdate (upsert) in the job so concurrent inserts collapse to one row
Wrap findFirst+create in a $transaction with isolationLevel:'Serializable' so the second writer's phantom read forces a serialisation failure and skips the create
Use an advisory lock keyed by appointmentId before the findFirst check so only one worker instance processes each appointment at a time
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: CompleteAppointment: same lost-update pattern as RescheduleAppointment — concurrent cancel can be silently overwritten
findings.businessRule: A cancellation of an appointment must not be silently reversed by a concurrent completion that loaded the entity before the cancellation committed.
invalidStates: Appointment status reverts from CANCELLED back to COMPLETED after the complete-use-case upsert wins
cancelledAt and cancellationReason fields are cleared by the complete upsert because the in-memory entity loaded before the cancel does not carry those fields

save() upserts with WHERE { id } only — no version or updatedAt precondition. A concurrent CancelAppointmentUseCase committing between load and save on the complete path will be silently overwritten, reverting a CANCELLED appointment back to COMPLETED.
No version column on Appointment model; updatedAt is @updatedAt (server-assigned) and is not used as a precondition in any upsert call.

findings.existingMitigations: none found
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add version column (Int @default(0)) to Appointment model; update WHERE id AND version=loadedVersion; throw conflict if rows_affected=0. Alternatively wrap load+save in withTransactionOptions({ isolationLevel: 'RepeatableRead' }).

Wrap CompleteAppointmentUseCase.execute() in withTransactionOptions({ isolationLevel: 'RepeatableRead' }) so a concurrent cancel commit causes a serialisation failure

findings.title: Contact/Lead concurrent create — check-then-act email uniqueness
findings.businessRule: At most one Contact (or Lead) per (tenantId, email) pair may exist; concurrent creates with the same email must not both succeed.
invalidStates: Two Contact rows with same (tenantId, email) — prevented by DB constraint but results in an opaque error instead of a controlled duplicate response
Caller receives PersistenceError and retries, creating another race window
Auto-merge is triggered by the second concurrent request seeing the first's newly created row, leading to an unintended merge of a freshly created contact

evidence.note: existsByEmail check runs outside any transaction; save runs in a separate Prisma call. Two concurrent requests both see 'false' before either commits.
save() uses upsert({ where: { id } }) — the id is a new cuid() so the upsert always creates; no tenant-scoped unique guard on the email at this callsite.
existsByEmail queries contact.count({ where: { email } }) — no tenantId filter, discussed separately in RACE-DEDUP-05.
@@unique([tenantId, email]) exists — DB will reject the second concurrent insert with a unique-constraint violation that bubbles as a generic PersistenceError, not a typed duplicate-email error.
Identical pattern: existsByEmail then lead create; Lead schema has @@unique([email, tenantId]) at schema line 472.
Router calls contactService.createContact after duplicate check; no retry or idempotent-create path on constraint violation.

findings.existingMitigations: @@unique([tenantId, email]) on Contact (schema.prisma:643) and @@unique([email, tenantId]) on Lead (schema.prisma:472) prevent double-inserts at the DB layer. The constraint error does propagate, but it surfaces as PersistenceError('Failed to save contact') — the caller cannot distinguish it from an infrastructure failure.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: @@unique([tenantId, email]) already present — no new constraint needed. Fix is to catch the P2002 Prisma error code and return a typed DuplicateEmailError from the repository.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ tenantId: fc.uuid(), email: fc.emailAddress(), firstName: fc.string(), lastName: fc.string() }) used as concurrent input to two createContact calls
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: existsByEmail(email) → false; T2: existsByEmail(email) → false; T1: upsert(newId1); T2: upsert(newId2) → DB unique violation on T2
T1: checkForCreate → proceed; T2: checkForCreate → proceed; T1: createContact; T2: createContact → constraint error
fixStrategyCandidates: Catch Prisma error P2002 in PrismaContactRepository.save() and throw a typed DuplicateEmailError that ContactService maps to ValidationError('already exists')
Replace existsByEmail + save with a Prisma createOrThrow pattern that relies solely on the DB constraint for atomicity
findings.verificationReason: packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaContactRepository.ts:78-82 uses upsert({ where: { id } }) — new cuid() so always creates. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:643 @@unique([tenantId, email]) will reject the second concurrent insert with a Prisma P2002 error. However packages/application/src/services/ContactService.ts:103-106 catches ALL errors and re-throws as a generic PersistenceError('Failed to save contact'), making it impossible for the caller (apps/api/src/modules/contact/contact.router.ts:601-608) to distinguish a duplicate-email constraint violation from an infrastructure failure. The caller then retries, creating a new race window. No P2002 handler exists in PrismaContactRepository.save(). The duplicate-rows outcome is blocked by the DB constraint, but the opaque-error path is a real High severity issue because callers cannot distinguish infra failures from legitimate duplicates and will surface the wrong error to the user (or retry into another race).

findings.title: Inbound lead dedup — check-then-act on submissionId tag without transaction
findings.businessRule: Each portal submission (identified by submissionId) must produce at most one Lead row; retried portal webhooks must be idempotent.
invalidStates: Two concurrent POST requests with same submissionId + same email both pass the findFirst check before either commits, then both call createLead — the second fails on unique constraint but the error path does a findFirst without the submissionId tag, potentially returning a different lead
Portal retries same submissionId with slightly different email (data normalisation variation) → two Lead rows with same logical submission

evidence.note: findFirst({ where: { tenantId, email, tags: { has: submissionTag } } }) runs outside any transaction, then createLead is called separately.
leadService.createLead is called after the dedup check with no transaction wrapping the check+create pair.

findings.title: PrismaContactRepository.existsByEmail and findByEmail lack tenantId scope — cross-tenant false positives block contact creation
findings.businessRule: Email uniqueness is tenant-scoped; a contact email valid in tenant B must not be blocked by an existing contact with the same email in tenant A.
invalidStates: Tenant B cannot create a contact with email X because tenant A already has a contact with email X — blocked by existsByEmail returning true across tenants
updateContactEmail for tenant B's contact falsely detects a conflict against tenant A's contact with the same email

evidence.note: existsByEmail queries contact.count({ where: { email: email.value } }) — no tenantId filter. Returns true if any tenant has that email.
findByEmail queries contact.findFirst({ where: { email: email.value } }) — again no tenantId. Returns the first matching row from any tenant.
createContact calls existsByEmail without passing tenantId; the result causes a false-positive block when another tenant has registered the same email.
updateContactEmail calls findByEmail and checks existingContact.id.value !== contactId — but the returned contact may belong to a different tenant, making the ownership check meaningless.
@@unique([tenantId, email]) — the uniqueness invariant is tenant-scoped at the DB level, contradicting the global query in existsByEmail.

findings.existingMitigations: None found. The DB schema correctly scopes uniqueness to (tenantId, email) but the service-layer check is global. In a single-tenant deployment this is invisible; in a multi-tenant SaaS it is a blocking cross-tenant interference bug.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: No new DB constraint needed — @@unique([tenantId, email]) already correct. Fix: add tenantId parameter to ContactRepository.existsByEmail and findByEmail ports, pass it through from ContactService.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.tuple(fc.uuid(), fc.uuid()).filter(([a,b]) => a !== b) as (tenantA, tenantB) paired with fc.emailAddress() to prove the same email can be created in both tenants

Use Prisma findUnique({ where: { tenantId_email: { tenantId, email } } }) which exploits the @@unique index directly

No existing mitigation.

findings.title: Lead convertLead — non-atomic two-step write (contactRepository.save then leadRepository.save) with no transaction
findings.businessRule: Lead conversion must atomically create the contact and mark the lead CONVERTED; a partial write (contact created, lead status not updated) must never persist.

lead.convert() sets status=CONVERTED on the in-memory entity. The guard `if (this.isConverted) return fail()` at line 317 is NOT DB-level — two concurrent callers both do findById, both get status=QUALIFIED, both call .convert() without error, both proceed to the two-step save.

findings.title: ensureCustomer has a read-check-write on stripeCustomerId — concurrent double-submit creates duplicate Stripe customers

stripeCustomerId String? @unique — the DB constraint prevents data corruption but does not prevent the dangling Stripe customer object.

Both proceed to stripe.createCustomer() (lines 911-918), creating two distinct Stripe customer objects. The @unique constraint on User.stripeCustomerId (schema.prisma:319) is the only guard — it makes the second prisma.user.update at line 928 throw P2002, preventing a second customer ID from being persisted. However, the orphan Stripe customer object (cus_B) is never cleaned up, violating the 'at most one Stripe customer per user' invariant at the Stripe layer. The second concurrent caller also receives an unhandled P2002 exception. Severity is downgraded from Critical to High because the DB stays consistent (only one stripeCustomerId persisted) and the dangling Stripe orphan is recoverable, but the P2002 is surfaced as a 500 to the user and the orphan constitutes a billing data inconsistency. No conditional updateMany or advisory lock exists.

findings.title: updateSubscription syncs modules to the pre-change DB plan because Workspace.plan is never updated in this path
findings.businessRule: When a tenant upgrades their subscription, the module set must be synced to the new plan tier, not the old one.
invalidStates: Tenant upgrades STARTER→PROFESSIONAL via updateSubscription; Workspace.plan stays STARTER; sync re-enables only STARTER modules; LEGAL never becomes available until a webhook fires
If webhook never fires (Stripe misconfiguration, network drop), the tenant remains on STARTER module access indefinitely despite paying for PROFESSIONAL

evidence.note: After stripe.updateSubscription succeeds, getTenantPlan() reads Workspace.plan from DB. But this field is never updated in updateSubscription — it stays at the old value.
syncModulesToPlan(user.tenantId, plan) is called with the stale plan tier from DB, not the plan derived from the new Stripe priceId.
Workspace.plan is the authoritative plan field. No code in billing.router.ts updates it when updateSubscription fires.
Only handleSubscriptionWebhook reads planTier from Stripe price metadata and could update modules to the correct tier — but Workspace.plan itself is still not updated there either.

findings.existingMitigations: none found
findings.requiredDbConstraints: No new DB constraint needed. Workspace.plan must be updated transactionally alongside syncModulesToPlan in updateSubscription, or the sync must derive the plan from the Stripe priceId rather than re-reading the DB.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ fromPlan: fc.constantFrom('STARTER','PROFESSIONAL'), toPlan: fc.constantFrom('PROFESSIONAL','ENTERPRISE','CUSTOM'), priceId: fc.string() }) — assert getEnabledModules post-sync includes all toPlan modules

getTenantPlan reads Workspace.plan = STARTER

findings.verificationReason: Confirmed at apps/api/src/modules/billing/billing.router.ts:708-716. After stripe.updateSubscription() succeeds at line 694, the code calls moduleAccess.getTenantPlan(user.tenantId) at line 710 which reads Workspace.plan from the database (via getTenantPlan in PrismaTenantModuleRepository.ts:68-87). A full-text search for 'workspace.*update.*plan', 'plan.*update.*workspace' in apps/api/src finds no write to Workspace.plan in the updateSubscription path. The plan field (schema.prisma:3197) is never updated here. syncModulesToPlan is thus called with the pre-change stale plan tier. The correct planTier is derivable from the priceId metadata (as handleSubscriptionWebhook correctly does at lines 1517-1522), but updateSubscription skips that derivation and re-reads stale DB state instead. This is a logic bug with a concurrency dimension: the correct plan is in Stripe's response but the DB is not updated before the sync. No mitigation found.

findings.title: syncModulesToPlan is additive-only — plan downgrades never disable above-plan module override rows
findings.businessRule: When a tenant downgrades to a lower plan tier, modules not included in the new tier must have their override rows set to enabled=false; a downgrade must not silently preserve access to modules the tenant no longer pays for.
invalidStates: Tenant downgrades ENTERPRISE→STARTER; LEGAL, ANALYTICS, AI_INTELLIGENCE override rows remain enabled=true; getEnabledModules returns all six modules indefinitely
Tenant cancels subscription; syncModulesToPlan(STARTER) called; all non-CORE_CRM enterprise rows still enabled; tenant retains full paid access

getEnabledModules merges plan defaults with overrides. With Workspace.plan still at STARTER (RACE-ENTIT-05) and override rows still enabled=true, every above-plan module remains accessible.
handleSubscriptionWebhook correctly passes the new planTier to syncModulesToPlan, but syncModulesToPlan does not disable the old plan's excess modules, making downgrade events ineffective.

findings.title: Invoice.recalculateTotals silently skips failures, leaving totals stale after a failed add/subtract
findings.businessRule: After any addLineItem or removeLineItem call, Invoice.totalAmount must equal sum(lineItem.total) + taxRate×subtotal, and amountDue must equal totalAmount - amountPaid + amountRefunded.
invalidStates: After removeLineItem(0), totalAmount still equals old value if recalculate silently fails
amountDue < 0 is impossible because subtract fails, but amountDue could be stale/too-high after a partial failure

evidence.note: recalculateTotals uses if(addResult.isSuccess){ subtotal=... } — failures are silently ignored. If any Money.add fails (e.g. currency mismatch injected via reconstitute), subtotal stays at Money.zero and totalAmount/amountDue are not updated, leaving a stale invariant with no error propagated to the caller.
addLineItem returns Result<void> but calls recalculateTotals which itself returns void. A partial recalculation failure is invisible to the caller that received Result.ok.
amountDue recalculation: afterPaidResult.subtract may fail if amountPaid > totalAmount after item removal, leaving amountDue at its pre-call value.

findings.existingMitigations: All factory-path line items are forced to the same currency (Invoice.ts:299 passes currency to LineItem.create), reducing the currency-mismatch risk in normal flow. However reconstitute() (line 396) accepts arbitrary InvoiceProps without re-validating currency consistency.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — pure domain invariant
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.record({description:fc.string({minLength:1}),quantity:fc.integer({min:1,max:100}),unitPriceCents:fc.integer({min:0,max:1_000_000}),type:fc.constantFrom('SUBSCRIPTION','ONE_TIME','USAGE','CREDIT','DISCOUNT')}),{minLength:1,maxLength:10}); fc.float({min:0,max:100}) for taxRate
commandSequenceCandidates: Invoice.create(props) → addLineItem(item) → assert totalAmount.cents === expected_sum_with_tax
Invoice.create(props) → removeLineItem(0) → assert totalAmount.cents reflects remaining items
create → addLineItem → removeLineItem(same index) — assert idempotency: totalAmount returns to original
fixStrategyCandidates: Change recalculateTotals to return Result<void, ...> and propagate failure through addLineItem/removeLineItem
Validate all line items share the same currency in reconstitute()
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed at packages/domain/src/crm/billing/Invoice.ts:656-679 — recalculateTotals() returns void and silently ignores Money.add failures via if(addResult.isSuccess) guards. Specifically: subtotal accumulation skips any line-item add failure (line 660), totalAmount is only updated if subtotal+totalTax succeeds (line 668), and amountDue is only updated if totalAmount-amountPaid succeeds (line 674). Invoice.reconstitute() at line 396 passes raw InvoiceProps without currency validation, so mixed-currency props injected from persistence cause partial recalculation failures invisible to addLineItem/removeLineItem callers which receive Result.ok. High severity confirmed — invoice totals are billing-critical.

findings.title: Invoice state machine allows processRefund on OPEN (partially paid) invoices but has no property test for the amountDue invariant across multi-step pay/refund sequences
findings.businessRule: At all times: amountDue.cents + amountPaid.cents - amountRefunded.cents === totalAmount.cents.
invalidStates: amountPaid + amountDue - amountRefunded !== totalAmount (accounting invariant broken)
paymentStatus === 'PAID' while amountDue.cents > 0

evidence.note: recordPayment: amountPaid += amount; amountDue -= amount. This is correct but no test verifies the invariant across interleaved recordPayment + processRefund calls.

processRefund: amountRefunded += amount; amountDue += amount. Combining with recordPayment: totalAmount should always equal amountPaid + amountDue - amountRefunded. A model-based test with arbitrary sequences would confirm this.
processRefund allows OPEN status (not just PAID), meaning a partial-payment→partial-refund loop is valid. The status/paymentStatus logic in that path is complex and untested end-to-end.

findings.existingMitigations: Individual guard checks exist (line 446: amount > amountDue → fail; line 537: amount > maxRefundable → fail). These prevent overdraft individually but do not provide a model-level invariant guarantee.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — pure domain invariant
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.oneof(fc.record({type:fc.constant('pay'),amount:fc.integer({min:1,max:1000})}), fc.record({type:fc.constant('refund'),amount:fc.integer({min:1,max:1000}),reason:fc.constantFrom('CUSTOMER_REQUEST','BILLING_ERROR')})),{minLength:1,maxLength:20})

create → issue → recordPayment(full) → processRefund(full) → assert status=OPEN, amountDue=totalAmount
create → issue → recordPayment(partial) → recordPayment(remainder) → assert status=PAID, amountDue=0

findings.title: Concurrent session creation bypasses enforceSessionLimit via shared module-level Map
findings.businessRule: A user may never hold more than maxConcurrentSessions (default 3) valid sessions simultaneously.

findings.title: Concurrent backup-code regeneration can cause two valid code sets to exist simultaneously
findings.businessRule: After regeneration, exactly one set of backup codes must be valid; old codes must be atomically invalidated.
invalidStates: User A and user B (same user, two browser tabs) each receive a different valid backup-code set; one set is immediately invalidated by the other's write

evidence.note: getBackupCodes: reads settings via getUserMfaSettings (which checks mfaSettingsCache first), spreads them, then calls saveUserMfaSettings with new hashedCodes. Two concurrent requests each read the same (or no) settings, generate independent code sets, and both upsert — last write wins, but both responses return different plaintext codes, so a user receives codes that are immediately overwritten.
regenerateBackupCodes has the same pattern: reads settings, verifies TOTP, generates new codes, saves. A second concurrent request with a valid TOTP from a 30s window can race the first write.
saveUserMfaSettings writes to mfaSettingsCache before the DB upsert, so a concurrent read on the same process can see the cache from request A while request B is still mid-flight to DB.
UserMfaSettings.userId has @unique — DB upsert is safe from duplicate rows, but does not prevent the last-write-wins lost-update on backupCodes.

Both getBackupCodes (auth.router.ts:942) and regenerateBackupCodes (auth.router.ts:1087) follow the pattern: await getUserMfaSettings (DB round-trip at mfa.service.ts:651-673), generate new codes, then await saveUserMfaSettings (DB upsert at mfa.service.ts:689). Two concurrent calls both await the DB read, both receive the same settings object, both generate independent plaintext code sets, and both await the DB upsert. Because UserMfaSettings.userId is @unique (schema.prisma:374) the upsert does not create a duplicate row, but the last writer wins on the backupCodes column. The first caller receives a plaintext code set that is immediately overwritten by the second upsert — those codes are now invalid in DB while the user holds them. No optimistic locking, no version field, no per-user mutex protects the read-generate-write span. findings.title: getUserRBACRoles and applyUserPermissionOverrides read role+permission data without tenantId filter, enabling cross-tenant permission leakage under shared userId space
findings.businessRule: A user's effective permission set must only include role assignments and overrides belonging to their current tenant.
