evidence.note: getUserRBACRoles queries userRoleAssignment with only { userId, expiresAt } in the WHERE clause — no tenantId filter. UserRoleAssignment.tenantId exists (schema.prisma:2615) and is indexed but is never used in the read path.
applyUserPermissionOverrides queries userPermission with only { userId, expiresAt } — again no tenantId filter. UserPermission.tenantId exists (schema.prisma:2638) but is not used in reads.

UserRoleAssignment has tenantId (non-nullable) and @@unique([userId, roleId]) — the unique constraint does not scope per tenant, meaning a user with userId=X in tenantA and tenantB shares the same role assignment rows.

In practice all users have unique IDs (Supabase UUIDs) so cross-tenant sharing via userId collision is unlikely, but the missing WHERE tenantId = $tenantId means future multi-tenant rows would all be visible to getPermissionsWithDB.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add tenantId to the WHERE clause in getUserRBACRoles and applyUserPermissionOverrides. Consider replacing @@unique([userId, roleId]) with @@unique([userId, roleId, tenantId]) to allow tenant-scoped role assignments.

fixStrategyCandidates: Add tenantId parameter to getUserRBACRoles and applyUserPermissionOverrides; propagate from getPermissionsWithDB caller

findings.verificationReason: Confirmed real. getUserRBACRoles (rbac.ts:436-444) queries userRoleAssignment with only { userId, expiresAt } — no tenantId filter. applyUserPermissionOverrides (rbac.ts:541-547) queries userPermission with only { userId, expiresAt } — no tenantId filter. Both UserRoleAssignment (schema.prisma:2615) and UserPermission (schema.prisma:2638) have a non-nullable tenantId column, confirming the intent of tenant scoping. The @@unique([userId, roleId]) constraint at schema.prisma:2623 lacks tenantId, so it is currently impossible for the same user to have different role assignments per tenant (the upsert key collides). While the current User model has a single tenantId (schema.prisma:342) reducing immediate cross-tenant collision risk, the read queries still aggregate all tenant assignments into one permission set — a design that will silently leak permissions if the data model evolves to support multi-tenant user membership. The missing WHERE tenantId = ? is a concrete, verifiable code gap, not a speculative future risk.

findings.title: OutboxEventBusAdapter.publish: findFirst-then-create outside transaction allows duplicate outbox rows
findings.businessRule: A domain event with the same idempotency key must appear at most once in the outbox.
invalidStates: Two concurrent publish() calls with the same eventId both return null from findFirst and both succeed in create, inserting two PENDING rows with the same idempotency key.
Downstream poller processes both rows, emitting duplicate side-effects (e.g., two lead-scored notifications, two billing charges).

evidence.note: findFirst checks metadata JSONB path for idempotencyKey (line 66-78), then create (line 83) — two separate DB round-trips with no enclosing transaction and no DB-level unique constraint on the key.

DomainEvent model has only @@index entries, zero @@unique constraints. No unique index on metadata->>'idempotencyKey'. ADR-011 schema spec at line 105 listed idempotency_key as a column with a UNIQUE INDEX but this was never implemented in the actual Prisma model.
Duplicate-skip test exercises the mock path only — findFirst returns a hardcoded value; no real-DB concurrent test exists.

findings.existingMitigations: Application-level findFirst guard at OutboxEventBusAdapter.ts:66-78, but no DB-enforced constraint. TOCTOU window between findFirst and create is unguarded.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: @@unique([tenantId, metadata]) is not directly expressible for JSONB paths in Prisma; the fix is a generated column + unique index on domain_events(tenantId, (metadata->>'idempotencyKey')) or wrapping the findFirst+create in a $transaction with serializable isolation.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ eventType: fc.constantFrom('lead.created','opportunity.won'), aggregateId: fc.uuid(), eventId: fc.uuid() }) — run N concurrent publish() calls with the same eventId via Promise.all on two separate PrismaClient connections.
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: findFirst(idempotencyKey) → T2: findFirst(idempotencyKey) → T1: create → T2: create (both succeed)
T1: findFirst(null) → T1: create → T2: findFirst(null, read committed) → T2: create
fixStrategyCandidates: Wrap findFirst+create in a $transaction({ isolationLevel: Prisma.TransactionIsolationLevel.Serializable }) inside publish().
Add a PostgreSQL generated/functional UNIQUE index on (tenant_id, (metadata->>'idempotencyKey')) via a raw migration.
Switch to prisma.domainEvent.upsert() keyed on a unique field, after adding that unique field to the schema.
Use createMany({ skipDuplicates: true }) as described in ADR-011 lines 273-276, which requires the unique index to be present first.
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. DomainEvent model (packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:1560-1586) has only @@index entries and zero @@unique constraints — no DB-level guard on (tenantId, metadata->>'idempotencyKey'). The idempotencyKey @unique field visible at schema.prisma:4843 belongs to LeadConversionAudit, not DomainEvent. OutboxEventBusAdapter.publish() (packages/adapters/src/events/OutboxEventBusAdapter.ts:66-102) issues findFirst then create as two independent Prisma calls; no $transaction wrapper exists on the single-event path. Two concurrent publish() calls with the same eventId both observe null from findFirst under READ COMMITTED and both succeed in create, inserting two PENDING rows. The duplicate-skip test at OutboxEventBusAdapter.impl.test.ts:249-256 is entirely mock-based and cannot detect this.

findings.title: OutboxPoller.processEvent: dispatch-then-markAsPublished is non-atomic — crash window causes duplicate dispatch
findings.businessRule: An outbox event dispatched to handlers must be marked PROCESSED in the same atomic unit; a crash between dispatch and acknowledgement must not cause double-dispatch.
invalidStates: Poller claims row, dispatches event (side-effect delivered), process crashes before markAsPublished; next poll cycle re-dispatches same event.
markAsPublished itself throws a transient DB error after dispatch; scheduleRetry puts the row back to PENDING with incremented retryCount, causing re-dispatch.

evidence.note: processEvent calls dispatcher.dispatch(event) at line 203 then repository.markAsPublished(event.id) at line 206 as sequential awaits with no wrapping transaction. If the process crashes or markAsPublished throws after dispatch succeeds, the row stays PENDING and will be re-dispatched on the next poll cycle.
poll() reschedules itself via setTimeout after processPendingEvents completes; any pending rows still in PENDING after a partial failure will be fetched again.

fetchPendingEvents uses FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED (line 171) which correctly serialises concurrent pollers, but the lock is released as soon as the SQL statement ends — it does NOT hold through the application-level dispatch.
Concurrent Access test only simulates SKIP LOCKED behaviour with a mock returning empty on second call — no real-DB test exercises the crash window.

findings.existingMitigations: FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED in fetchPendingEvents prevents two pollers from claiming the same row simultaneously, but provides no protection after the lock is released at statement end. No PROCESSING status transition is set before dispatch in pollOutbox.ts (the status only moves to PROCESSED after markAsPublished at line 183-191 of PrismaOutboxRepository.ts).
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add a status transition to PROCESSING immediately before dispatch inside a transaction (compare-and-swap: UPDATE … WHERE status='PENDING' AND id=? SET status='PROCESSING'). If dispatch succeeds, transition to PROCESSED. If PROCESSING rows are found on restart, use a lock-timeout to dead-letter stale ones.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ eventId: fc.uuid(), dispatchSucceeds: fc.boolean(), markPublishedThrows: fc.boolean() }) — model-based test that exercises all four combinations of (dispatch, markPublished) success/failure.
commandSequenceCandidates: fetchPending → dispatch (succeeds) → markAsPublished (throws) → scheduleRetry → fetchPending → dispatch (duplicate)
fetchPending → dispatch (succeeds) → process crash → restart → fetchPending → dispatch (duplicate)
fixStrategyCandidates: Claim the row by transitioning to PROCESSING status atomically (conditional UPDATE WHERE status='PENDING') before dispatching; on success mark PROCESSED; on failure roll back to PENDING.
Make all handlers truly idempotent with their own deduplication table keyed on (eventId, handlerName) — eliminates the duplicate-effect risk even when re-dispatched.
Use a saga/two-phase commit: write handler result to DB in same transaction as status update.
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. pollOutbox.ts:203-206 awaits dispatcher.dispatch(event) then repository.markAsPublished(event.id) as sequential calls with no enclosing transaction and no prior status transition to PROCESSING. PrismaOutboxRepository.ts:132-172 issues FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED which holds only through the SQL statement end, not through the application-level dispatch. If the process crashes or markAsPublished throws after dispatch, the row remains PENDING and the next poll cycle re-dispatches the same event. No markAsProcessing / compare-and-swap exists anywhere in PrismaOutboxRepository.ts (confirmed by grep for claimEvent/PROCESSING/markProcessing — zero matches in the repository implementation). Concurrent Access test at PrismaOutboxRepository.test.ts:307-338 uses mocked second-call-empty semantics and does not exercise the crash window.

findings.title: OutboxEventBusAdapter.publishAll: findFirst-then-create inside withTransaction uses READ COMMITTED isolation, not SERIALIZABLE — phantom-insert race still possible
findings.businessRule: publishAll must guarantee at-most-one outbox row per idempotency key even under concurrent batch publishes.
invalidStates: Two concurrent publishAll() calls with overlapping eventIds both run findFirst inside their own READ COMMITTED transactions, both observe no existing row, both create a row — duplicate PENDING rows despite the transaction wrapper.

evidence.note: publishAll wraps findFirst+create in withTransaction but withTransaction at packages/db/src/client.ts:178-184 calls prisma.$transaction(fn) with no isolationLevel option, defaulting to READ COMMITTED. Under READ COMMITTED, two concurrent transactions both see no existing row for the same idempotencyKey, both proceed to create, and both succeed — the transaction wrapper provides atomicity per-statement but not snapshot isolation across the findFirst→create pair.
withTransaction(fn) calls (prisma as any).$transaction(fn) with no second options argument, so isolationLevel defaults to READ COMMITTED. No SERIALIZABLE or REPEATABLE READ is requested.

findings.existingMitigations: withTransaction provides a transaction boundary but at READ COMMITTED isolation, which does not prevent concurrent phantom inserts on a JSONB path filter.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Unique index on (tenantId, (metadata->>'idempotencyKey')) OR use $transaction({ isolationLevel: Prisma.TransactionIsolationLevel.Serializable }).
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.record({ eventId: fc.uuid(), eventType: fc.constantFrom('lead.created','opportunity.won') }), { minLength: 2, maxLength: 5 }) — run two concurrent publishAll() calls sharing at least one event via Promise.all with two separate PrismaClient connections.
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: BEGIN (READ COMMITTED) → T1: findFirst(key)=null → T2: BEGIN → T2: findFirst(key)=null → T1: create → T1: COMMIT → T2: create → T2: COMMIT (both rows exist)
fixStrategyCandidates: Pass { isolationLevel: Prisma.TransactionIsolationLevel.Serializable } to $transaction in withTransaction or add a withTransactionSerializable helper.
Add the unique generated-column index on domain_events as described in RACE-WEBHO-01 fix candidates — makes create fail on duplicate and removes the need for the pre-check entirely.

findings.title: PrismaOutboxRepository.fetchPendingEvents: FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED is issued outside a long-running transaction — lock released after statement, not after dispatch
findings.businessRule: A row claimed by FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED must remain locked until the processing result (PROCESSED or FAILED) is committed, preventing a second poller from claiming it during the dispatch window.
invalidStates: Poller A fetches row (lock held during $queryRaw, released immediately after). Poller B runs fetchPendingEvents within 100ms — row is still PENDING, lock is no longer held by A. Poller B also gets the row. Both pollers dispatch the event.

evidence.note: fetchPendingEvents issues FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED via $queryRaw inside an implicit autocommit transaction (no explicit $transaction wrapper). PostgreSQL releases the row lock when the statement (and thus the implicit transaction) commits — immediately after fetchPendingEvents returns. A second poller calling fetchPendingEvents 1ms later will see the row as unlocked if status is still PENDING and can claim it.

processEvent() calls fetchPendingEvents (via processPendingEvents at line 180) before processEvent; the row lock from FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED is held only during the $queryRaw, not across the subsequent dispatcher.dispatch() (line 203) and markAsPublished (line 206) calls. A second poller can re-fetch the same PENDING row in the gap.

findings.existingMitigations: FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED is present but its lock scope is limited to the statement boundary. There is no explicit BEGIN/COMMIT wrapping the fetch+dispatch+mark trio, and no PROCESSING status is written before releasing the lock.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Wrap fetchPendingEvents inside an explicit $transaction that stays open until markAsPublished is committed, OR write status=PROCESSING atomically before releasing the lock so a second poller skips the row on its WHERE status='PENDING' filter.
findings.generatedInputs: Two separate PrismaClient connections running fetchPendingEvents simultaneously against a real postgres:15-alpine container; assert each row appears in exactly one result set.
commandSequenceCandidates: A: $queryRaw FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED → A: lock released (autocommit) → B: $queryRaw FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED → B: gets same row → A: dispatch → B: dispatch (duplicate)
fixStrategyCandidates: Add a markAsProcessing(eventId) step that executes UPDATE domain_events SET status='PROCESSING' WHERE id=? AND status='PENDING' immediately after fetching, releasing the FOR UPDATE lock; pollers filter WHERE status='PENDING' so won't re-claim PROCESSING rows.

Wrap the entire fetch+dispatch+mark sequence in a single explicit $transaction (requires the lock to be held for the transaction duration — use $transaction with isolationLevel REPEATABLE READ or Serializable and keep it short).

Replace $queryRaw with an UPDATE ... SET status='PROCESSING' WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM domain_events WHERE status='PENDING' LIMIT ? FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) RETURNING * — atomically claims and transitions status in one statement.

findings.title: MaintenanceScheduler SLA-breach: findFirst-then-create without transaction creates duplicate notifications under concurrent runs
findings.businessRule: Each ticket SLA breach must produce exactly one notification per worker run; duplicate notifications must not be delivered to the assignee.
invalidStates: Ticket slaStatus = BREACHED but two BREACHED notifications exist for the same ticket in the same run window
slaBreachedAt set to two different timestamps on the same ticket
Assignee receives duplicate SLA breach push notifications

evidence.note: checkSLABreaches: reads tickets with slaStatus != BREACHED, then calls ticket.update + notification.create in a plain loop with no transaction. Two concurrent MaintenanceScheduler instances (horizontal scaling or overlapping intervals) can both read the same un-breached ticket, both call update, and both insert a notification.
Notification model has no @@unique constraint on (tenantId, sourceId, sourceType) — any number of duplicate rows can be inserted for the same ticket breach event.

Five setInterval loops are registered in start(). If checkSLABreaches() takes longer than slaCheckIntervalMs (60s), the next interval fires while the prior run is still in-flight. runSafe only swallows exceptions; it does not prevent overlapping execution.

findings.existingMitigations: The where clause `slaStatus: { not: 'BREACHED' }` narrows the query to un-breached rows, but the ticket.update and notification.create are NOT inside a $transaction, so two concurrent executions can both pass the read phase before either write commits.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add @@unique([tenantId, sourceId, sourceType]) to Notification model, OR wrap the findMany+update+create block in $transaction with serializable isolation.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ ticketId: fc.uuid(), slaResolutionDue: fc.date({ max: new Date() }), slaStatus: fc.constant('ON_TRACK'), assigneeId: fc.uuid(), tenantId: fc.uuid() }); drive two concurrent checkSLABreaches() calls against the same Prisma client
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: prisma.ticket.findMany → [ticket_A]; T2: prisma.ticket.findMany → [ticket_A]; T1: ticket.update(BREACHED); T2: ticket.update(BREACHED); T1: notification.create; T2: notification.create → duplicate notification
Single instance, interval fires at t=0 and t=60s, first run still processing at t=60s → overlap
fixStrategyCandidates: Wrap each ticket's update+notification.create in prisma.$transaction([...]) so the status flip and notification creation are atomic
Add a DB-level unique constraint on (tenantId, sourceId, sourceType) with an ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING upsert so concurrent inserts are idempotent
Use an optimistic conditional update: prisma.ticket.updateMany({ where: { id, slaStatus: { not: 'BREACHED' } }, data: { slaStatus: 'BREACHED' } }) and skip notification.create when count === 0
findings.verificationReason: apps/workers/events-worker/src/maintenance/scheduled-jobs.ts:156-183 — ticket.update + notification.create are two separate awaits outside any $transaction. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:4640-4649 confirms the Notification model has no @@unique on (tenantId, sourceId, sourceType); only plain @@index entries exist. Two concurrent MaintenanceScheduler instances (or an overlapping setInterval tick) can both pass the findMany filter before either update commits, then both update the ticket and both insert the notification. No existing mitigation prevents the duplicate notification row.

findings.title: RetryBudgetTracker: enqueueAIScoring calls canRetry() but never consumeRetry() — retry budget enforcement is permanently inoperative
findings.businessRule: The global retry budget must actually decrement on each enqueue so that the burst-protection ceiling is enforced within the sliding window.
invalidStates: A burst of 10,000 scoring enqueue calls within one window succeeds even though defaultBudget=100 should have stopped at 100
Thundering-herd from a deployment restart saturates the Redis scoring queue without any backpressure

evidence.note: enqueueAIScoring calls globalRetryBudget.canRetry(QUEUE_NAMES.AI_SCORING) to gate enqueue, but never calls globalRetryBudget.consumeRetry(). The budget.remaining counter is never decremented, so canRetry() always returns true and the guard is a no-op.

canRetry() reads budget.remaining > 0; consumeRetry() decrements it. They are separate calls by design. Only queue-factory.ts:226 references canRetry for AI_SCORING but consumeRetry is never called in queue-factory.ts.
Existing tests cover canRetry + consumeRetry in isolation but do NOT test enqueueAIScoring's interaction with the budget. The integration gap is invisible to current tests.

findings.existingMitigations: none found — consumeRetry is never called in the enqueue path
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — pure in-process fix: add globalRetryBudget.consumeRetry(QUEUE_NAMES.AI_SCORING) immediately after the canRetry guard passes
findings.generatedInputs: fc.integer({ min: 1, max: 500 }) for call count; assert that after N > defaultBudget (100) calls to enqueueAIScoring in the same window, at least (N - 100) of them were rejected — currently this property fails
commandSequenceCandidates: Call enqueueAIScoring 150 times within one windowMs; expect first 100 to succeed and next 50 to throw; currently all 150 succeed
fixStrategyCandidates: Add globalRetryBudget.consumeRetry(QUEUE_NAMES.AI_SCORING) at queue-factory.ts line 231 (immediately after the canRetry guard block)

Replace the canRetry+consumeRetry pair with a single atomic tryConsume() method that reads and decrements in one operation to eliminate the TOCTOU window between canRetry and consumeRetry under concurrent calls
findings.verificationReason: packages/platform/src/queues/queue-factory.ts:226-256 — globalRetryBudget.canRetry(QUEUE_NAMES.AI_SCORING) is called at line 226 but consumeRetry is nowhere in queue-factory.ts (confirmed by grep returning no matches). packages/platform/src/queues/retry-strategy.ts:187-203 shows canRetry reads budget.remaining while consumeRetry decrements it; without the latter the counter never moves. The burst-protection ceiling is completely non-functional. Upgraded from Medium to High: an unguarded thundering-herd on a deployment restart can saturate the Redis scoring queue with no backpressure, directly impacting AI service availability.

findings.title: DomainEvent outbox: markAsPublished uses a plain update with no status pre-check — concurrent poller instances can double-dispatch the same event
findings.businessRule: Each domain event in the outbox must be dispatched exactly once across all poller instances; double-dispatch causes duplicate side effects (duplicate notifications, double billing, etc.).
invalidStates: Same domain event dispatched twice to subscription-bridge handlers — e.g., LeadScored emitted twice, causing duplicate real-time notifications to connected clients
notificationId from enqueueNotification called twice for the same event — duplicate notification delivery to end users

evidence.note: fetchPendingEvents uses FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED — this is the correct pattern for a single PostgreSQL session. However, if the Prisma adapter-pg connection pool has multiple connections (default pool size can be > 1), or if two poller instances run on different pods, SKIP LOCKED only prevents two concurrent transactions from locking the SAME rows — it does not prevent poller-A from fetching event-E, marking it PROCESSING, then crashing before markAsPublished, and poller-B re-fetching it on the retry (status reverts to PENDING after lock release).

processPendingEvents fetches then processEvent dispatches then markAsPublished. If dispatch succeeds but markAsPublished throws (transient DB error), the event remains PENDING and will be re-dispatched on the next poll — causing a duplicate side-effect delivery with no idempotency key on the handler side.

findings.existingMitigations: FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED in fetchPendingEvents (packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaOutboxRepository.ts:171) prevents concurrent fetches within one DB session. However, the dispatch-then-mark sequence is not atomic — a crash between dispatch and markAsPublished causes at-least-once delivery with no dedup on the consumer side.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Move markAsPublished into the same transaction as a status-conditional update: UPDATE domain_events SET status='PROCESSED' WHERE id=$1 AND status='PROCESSING' — skip dispatch if update count = 0 (already processed by another instance)
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ eventId: fc.uuid(), eventType: fc.constantFrom('lead.scored', 'task.assigned') }); simulate dispatch succeeding then markAsPublished throwing; assert handler called exactly once across retry
commandSequenceCandidates: Poller fetches event-E (FOR UPDATE lock held); dispatch(event-E) succeeds; markAsPublished(event-E) throws DB error; lock released; next poll re-fetches event-E; dispatch(event-E) called again — duplicate side effect
fixStrategyCandidates: Wrap the dispatch+markAsPublished in a single DB transaction: use $transaction([dispatch-audit-write, markAsPublished]) so a crash rolls back both atomically, and add an idempotency check at the handler level
Add an explicit status transition PENDING→PROCESSING (atomic updateMany WHERE status=PENDING) before dispatch, and PROCESSING→PROCESSED after — skip dispatch if the PROCESSING claim fails (count=0)
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: AuditLogger singleton captures first PrismaClient — all subsequent requests bypass tenant-scoped prismaWithTenant extensions
findings.businessRule: Every audit write must be scoped to the correct tenant so cross-tenant audit data is impossible; writing through an unscoped PrismaClient connection bypasses the tenant middleware applied to `ctx.prismaWithTenant`.
invalidStates: Singleton initialized with tenant-A's prisma; tenant-B's audit writes go through tenant-A's connection pool, losing per-tenant RLS policies if they were applied at the adapter level
validateTenant returns true then tenant is deleted before auditLogEntry.create, causing FK violation that swallows the audit event silently (console.error only)

evidence.note: `let auditLoggerInstance: AuditLogger | null = null; getAuditLogger(prisma) { auditLoggerInstance ??= new AuditLogger(prisma, config); }` — module-level singleton; the first caller's `prisma` is captured forever.
`const auditLogger = getAuditLogger(ctx.prisma)` — passes the unscoped `ctx.prisma` (not `ctx.prismaWithTenant`), so even the first call uses the base client without row-level tenant filter.
`validateTenant` does an extra round-trip `prisma.tenant.findUnique` before each write — this TOCTOU read is not inside a transaction, so a tenant could be deleted between the check and the insert.

findings.existingMitigations: tenantId is explicitly written to every audit row; Prisma FK constraint on tenantId → tenants(id) prevents orphan rows if the tenant is deleted before the insert. The singleton issue is partially mitigated by explicit tenantId fields.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none additional — FK constraint already present; fix is architectural (pass prisma per-call not once to singleton)
findings.generatedInputs: fc.tuple(fc.uuidV4(), fc.uuidV4()) representing two different tenantIds racing to initialize the singleton
commandSequenceCandidates: race_initialization: call getAuditLogger(prismaA) and getAuditLogger(prismaB) concurrently; assert both writes use correct tenant context
validateThenDelete: validateTenant passes, then DELETE tenant row, then auditLogEntry.create — assert graceful error handling not data loss
fixStrategyCandidates: Make getAuditLogger() accept prisma as a per-call parameter (factory pattern) rather than caching the first instance
Inject AuditLogger from DI container per-request so each request gets the tenanted client
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed but severity downgraded from High to Medium. `index.ts:72` uses `??=` to capture the first caller's `prisma` forever. All callers pass `ctx.prisma` (never `ctx.prismaWithTenant`) — confirmed in opportunity.router.ts:723, auth.router.ts:369, task.router.ts:207, etc. However `createTenantScopedPrisma` only sets a PostgreSQL session variable for RLS; it does not filter rows by tenantId at the ORM level. The AuditLogger passes explicit `tenantId` in every row (writer.ts:88, security-handler.ts:66) and the Prisma FK constraint `tenantId → tenants(id)` prevents cross-tenant writes. The practical risk is not cross-tenant data leakage through the singleton but rather holding a stale connection reference and bypassing future RLS enforcement if it were added. The TOCTOU `validateTenant` TOCT read-then-write is real (writer.ts:43 then :87) but the FK constraint is the safety net. Severity Medium not High.

findings.title: Async AuditLogger buffer flush — non-atomic splice + Promise.all + unshift allows duplicate writes on concurrent flush triggers
findings.businessRule: Each buffered audit entry must be written to the database exactly once; concurrent flush invocations must not duplicate entries.
invalidStates: batchSize overflow flush starts, splices 100 entries, begins await; timer fires before await resolves, splices next 100 (or 0), returns; first flush fails, unshifts 100 entries back — but timer flush already completed some of them
Two simultaneous shutdown() calls each call flush(), both splice the same entries

evidence.note: `flush()`: `const toWrite = this.buffer.splice(0, batchSize)` removes entries then `await Promise.all(...)` writes them; on catch `this.buffer.unshift(...toWrite)` re-inserts. If a second flush fires while the first is awaiting, `splice` on the now-shorter buffer yields a different slice — but the catch path can reinsert entries already partially written.

`scheduleFlush()`: two code paths can call `flush()` — the `batchSize` threshold path (line 248) and the setTimeout path (line 252) — both can fire before the first flush completes because `isPolling` / in-flight guard is absent.
`private readonly buffer: AuditLogInput[] = []` and `flushTimer: NodeJS.Timeout | null` — these are the only guards; no in-flight Promise reference is tracked.

findings.existingMitigations: The `flushTimer` guard (`if (this.flushTimer) return`) prevents double-scheduling via the timer path, but does NOT prevent the batchSize-overflow path from calling flush() while a timer-triggered flush is in progress.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Adding a DB-level unique constraint on (eventId) in AuditLogEntry would turn duplicates into detectable errors rather than silent over-counting.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.record({ action: fc.constantFrom('CREATE','UPDATE','DELETE'), resourceId: fc.uuidV4() }), { minLength: 101, maxLength: 300 })
commandSequenceCandidates: concurrent_flush: enqueue 150 entries, trigger flush() twice simultaneously, assert exactly 150 DB writes
flush_during_shutdown: call log() 50 times then shutdown() concurrently, assert no duplicate rows
fixStrategyCandidates: Track in-flight flush with a Promise reference; if flush is already running, skip and rely on the caller to retry
Add a unique eventId to each buffered entry and use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING
Disable async mode by default (it is already default=false) and document the risk clearly
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. `scheduleFlush()` at audit-logger.ts:244-256: the `if (this.flushTimer) return` guard at :245 only prevents double-scheduling via the timer path. The batchSize-overflow path at :247-249 calls `flush().catch({})` and immediately returns WITHOUT setting `flushTimer`. So if `scheduleFlush()` is called again before the first overflow flush resolves (e.g. 200 items arrive in rapid succession), the guard is bypassed for each call that finds `buffer.length >= batchSize` and multiple concurrent `flush()` calls are in flight. `flush()` splices `batchSize` items at :221 — if two flushes fire concurrently, they splice different slices and write them in parallel, but the `catch` + `unshift` at :227 can re-queue items that were already partially written. However `config.async` defaults to `false` (:61), so this only activates when async mode is explicitly enabled. Severity Medium is appropriate.

findings.title: DurableAuditLogAdapter.logBatchEvents silently swallows errors inside the transaction loop — failed events are counted but their failure is never surfaced to the caller transaction rollback
findings.businessRule: A batch audit write failure must either roll back the entire batch or precisely identify and re-queue the failed events; silently incrementing a failureCount while the enclosing $transaction continues is incorrect.
invalidStates: 3 of 5 events fail DB write but transaction commits the other 2; caller sees failureCount=3 but has no event IDs to retry with
A cross-tenant event triggers CrossTenantViolationError mid-batch; earlier events in the same transaction are committed, later events are skipped — compliance record is incomplete

evidence.note: `logBatchEvents` wraps the entire loop in `prisma.$transaction`, but inside the loop each event's failure is caught at lines 227-233 and only increments `failureCount` — the catch prevents the transaction from aborting, so partial success is committed. The caller receives `successCount + failureCount = totalEvents` but has no way to re-drive the failed subset.

On failure the result is `{ eventId: randomUUID(), status: 'FAILED' }` — a brand-new random UUID is generated for the failed event, making it impossible to correlate the failure with the original event for retry.
Batch test only verifies the happy path (transactionCallCount === 1, successCount === 3); no test exercises partial failure.

findings.existingMitigations: The $transaction wrapper means DB writes that succeed are durably committed; the issue is the silent partial failure semantics, not data corruption per se.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none additional — fix is behavioral: either abort the entire transaction on first failure (all-or-nothing semantics) or collect failures without swallowing and return original event IDs.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.record({ eventType: fc.string(), tenantId: fc.constantFrom('t1','t2') }), { minLength: 2, maxLength: 10 }) with injected DB failures on specific indices

fixStrategyCandidates: Remove the try/catch inside the loop and let any error propagate to abort the whole transaction (all-or-nothing)

findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. `logBatchEvents` at DurableAuditLogAdapter.ts:169-236 wraps the loop in `prisma.$transaction` but individual event failures are silently caught at :227-233. The catch does NOT re-throw so the transaction is not aborted — Prisma's `$transaction` only rolls back if the callback throws or rejects. Events that succeeded before the failed one remain committed, and events after the failure are attempted with a fresh `randomUUID()` as the failure result. Critically, line :230 generates a new `randomUUID()` for the failure result rather than preserving the original event's data, making it impossible for callers to identify or retry the failed event. The existing mitigation is only that the `$transaction` wraps everything — but the internal catch defeats this. The auditor's 'missing-idempotency-key' label is imprecise; the actual pattern is partial-commit-semantics inside a transaction.

findings.title: PrismaOutboxRepository.fetchPendingEvents lacks PROCESSING status flip — row lock released after SELECT, allowing duplicate dispatch under crash-restart
findings.businessRule: Each domain event must be dispatched to the AuditEventHandler exactly once; concurrent restart while a batch is in-flight must not replay the same events.
invalidStates: Worker fetches batch, begins dispatching, crashes mid-batch; on restart the events are still PENDING and are dispatched again, causing duplicate AuditLogEntry rows
Horizontal scale with two worker processes: the lock from the $queryRaw SELECT is released as soon as the result set is returned; a second worker starting within that window can pick up the same rows

evidence.note: `fetchPendingEvents` uses `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` but does NOT update `status = PROCESSING` inside the same transaction. The lock is held only for the duration of the SELECT statement, not for the duration of event processing.
`mapPrismaStatus` maps `PROCESSING -> 'pending'` — the PROCESSING status is treated as pending for retry; but since it is never set by fetchPendingEvents, this mapping is dead code.
`processPendingEvents` calls `fetchPendingEvents` then iterates the results with `processEvent` serially — no status flip to PROCESSING between fetch and dispatch.

findings.existingMitigations: The `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` prevents two concurrent poll cycles from seeing the same rows simultaneously, so duplicate dispatch is only possible across process restarts (crash between fetch and markAsPublished), not within a single-process concurrent scenario. The severity is therefore Medium rather than Critical.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Add an atomic CTE or a separate UPDATE ... WHERE status='PENDING' RETURNING inside the same raw SQL transaction to flip status to PROCESSING atomically with the SELECT.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.record({ eventType: fc.string(), aggregateId: fc.uuidV4() }), { minLength: 1, maxLength: 20 })
commandSequenceCandidates: restartDuringDispatch: fetch batch, simulate crash after dispatch of first event, restart worker, assert second event not double-processed
twoPollers: two PrismaOutboxRepository instances fetch concurrently, assert no overlapping event IDs in the two result sets
fixStrategyCandidates: Replace the two-step SELECT + external update with a single CTE: `WITH locked AS (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) UPDATE domain_events SET status='PROCESSING' WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM locked) RETURNING *`
Add a unique idempotency key on AuditLogEntry derived from (eventId, action) with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING so duplicate dispatches are harmless
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. `fetchPendingEvents` at PrismaOutboxRepository.ts:132-175 issues `$queryRaw` with `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`. In Prisma 7, `$queryRaw` outside an explicit `$transaction` executes in an implicit auto-committed transaction that is released as soon as the result set is returned to Node. The lock is therefore held only for the SELECT statement duration, not for the event processing duration. After the raw query returns at :174, the lock is gone and another process can immediately select the same rows. `markAsPublished` is only called at pollOutbox.ts:206 after successful dispatch. On crash-restart, all fetched-but-not-marked rows are re-selected as PENDING and double-dispatched. For a horizontally-scaled two-worker scenario, the window between `$queryRaw` returning and `markAsPublished` completing allows overlap. The existing `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` is not ineffective — it prevents two simultaneous `$queryRaw` calls from seeing the same rows — but it provides no protection across the async processing gap.

findings.title: validateTenant TOCTOU in writer.ts and security-handler.ts — tenant deleted between SELECT and INSERT causes FK violation swallowed to console.error
findings.businessRule: If a tenant is deleted concurrently, the audit write must either complete atomically within the tenant's lifetime or fail loudly; silently swallowing the FK violation to console.error means the audit event is permanently lost.
invalidStates: validateTenant returns true; tenant row deleted (CASCADE triggers); auditLogEntry.create throws FK violation; fire-and-forget caller logs to console and discards — audit event permanently lost

evidence.note: `validateTenant` does `prisma.tenant.findUnique({ where: { id: tenantId }, select: { id: true } })` — a plain SELECT outside any transaction.
`const tenantValid = await validateTenant(...)` — if this returns true but the tenant is deleted before line 87's `prisma.auditLogEntry.create`, the INSERT throws a FK constraint violation.

`catch (error) { console.error('[AUDIT] Failed to write audit log:', error); throw error; }` — the error IS re-thrown, so audit loss only occurs if the caller silently ignores the rejection (fire-and-forget pattern used in opportunity.router.ts:733 `.catch(err => console.error(...))`). The same pattern in security-handler.ts:11-25 + 64 has the same TOCTOU window.
All audit calls are fire-and-forget: `auditLogger.logAction(...).catch(err => console.error(...))` — FK violation from TOCTOU deletion is silently swallowed at the call site.

findings.existingMitigations: The Prisma FK constraint on tenantId → tenants(id) with onDelete:Cascade means tenant deletion cascades to AuditLogEntry rows, so a tenant race during deletion would eventually clean up committed rows. However the FK violation on concurrent INSERT is not handled, and fire-and-forget callers discard the error.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none additional — FK already enforced; fix is to remove validateTenant pre-check entirely (rely on the FK for enforcement) and handle the FK error explicitly with re-throw or metric increment rather than fire-and-forget swallow
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ tenantId: fc.uuidV4() }) — interleaving: validateTenant → DELETE tenant → auditLogEntry.create
commandSequenceCandidates: toctou_tenant_delete: validateTenant passes, delete tenant row, call auditLogEntry.create — assert graceful handling not silent loss
fire_and_forget_swallow: wrap logAction in .catch(console.error), trigger FK violation — assert monitoring/alerting is triggered not just console output
fixStrategyCandidates: Remove the validateTenant pre-check; rely solely on the DB FK constraint; catch FK violation specifically (Prisma error code P2003) and re-throw a typed error so callers can decide whether to retry

findings.title: Concurrent bidirectional merge (A->B and B->A) — lost-update window before transaction
findings.businessRule: A contact merge is irreversible; concurrent merge requests for the same pair in opposite directions must not both proceed past the pre-flight read.

mergeInTransaction re-verifies both IDs inside the $transaction (findFirst inside tx). The second concurrent merge will throw CrossTenantOrNotFoundError when the secondary no longer exists — rolling back cleanly.

Prisma $transaction defaults to READ COMMITTED; no isolationLevel:'Serializable' is set. Under RC a concurrent merge (B->A) could read the secondary after T1's tx has deleted it but before the tx is visible, depending on connection pool timing.

Under READ COMMITTED (no isolationLevel set — confirmed by grep showing zero isolationLevel occurrences in PrismaContactRepository.ts), once T1's $transaction deletes secondary B, T2's findFirst for B inside its own concurrent $transaction will return null after T1 commits, causing CrossTenantOrNotFoundError and rollback. findings.title: Contact auto-merge post-create — contact created before merge executes, leaving a window for concurrent interactions on the soon-to-be-deleted row
findings.businessRule: When autoMergeOnExactEmail is enabled, the newly created contact must be merged into the primary before any other operation can reference it.

evidence.note: checkForCreate decides 'auto-merge', then contactService.createContact at line 601 creates the row, then applyAutoMerge at line 614 merges it. There is a commit gap between create (line 601) and merge (line 614) during which the new contactId is live.
auto-merge failure is swallowed with console.warn — if applyAutoMerge throws, the newly created contact is never merged and the duplicate persists.
applyAutoMerge delegates to deps.mergeContacts which throws if no dep is wired — an uncaught throw propagates through the router's catch block (line 620-622) and is swallowed.

findings.existingMitigations: The merge itself (mergeInTransaction) is atomic. The @@unique([tenantId, email]) constraint on Contact means the newly created row is visible but protected from further duplicate creates. The window is bounded to the time between the create commit and the merge commit.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: No new constraint needed. Fix: make auto-merge failure non-swallowable (throw TRPCError) or atomically prevent the create+merge split by checking the existing contact before any DB write and returning the existing ID directly.

commandSequenceCandidates: checkForCreate → auto-merge; createContact → commits newId; [concurrent webhook assigns task to newId]; applyAutoMerge → merges newId into primaryId; task now references deleted newId
checkForCreate → auto-merge; createContact → commits newId; applyAutoMerge throws (no dep wired) → caught/swallowed → duplicate persists

findings.title: Account model has no name/website uniqueness constraint — duplicate-detection is advisory only, concurrent creates bypass it entirely
findings.businessRule: Duplicate-detection for accounts must not allow two concurrent account-create requests with the same name/website to both succeed undetected.

Auto-link by domain (linkContactsByDomain) later links contacts to the wrong account if there are two accounts with the same domain

evidence.note: Account model has @@unique([tenantId, id]) and various indexes but NO @@unique([tenantId, name]) or @@unique([tenantId, website]). Concurrent creates of accounts with identical names will both succeed.
check() reads active rules, fetches candidates, evaluates deterministic rules — all outside any transaction. Two concurrent creates both see zero candidates before either commits.
ADR explicitly states accounts are NEVER auto-merged — only flagged. The flag notification is advisory; no DB constraint backs it up.

The entire check (loadActiveRules → fetchCandidates → evaluate → emitFlagNotification) is outside any transaction; there is no lock that would prevent a second concurrent create from bypassing the check.

The Promise.all fan-out at lines 155-172 commits each TenantModule upsert independently with no wrapping $transaction. The trailing getEnabledModules() call at line 175 is a separate read, so a concurrent disableModule or toggleModule that commits between the last upsert and that read will produce a partial snapshot. findings.title: Process-level billingCache Map is not coherent across replicas — post-mutation stale entitlement reads persist up to 5 minutes
findings.businessRule: After a module toggle or plan change, all API replicas must serve the updated entitlement state within the SLA; stale entitlement data must not persist beyond the cache TTL on any replica.
invalidStates: Replica A processes toggleModule(LEGAL, enabled=false), invalidates its local cache
Replica B still serves LEGAL as enabled from its 5-minute cached entry
User routed to Replica B gains unauthorized LEGAL module access for up to 5 minutes

evidence.note: billingCache is a module-level Map — process-scoped singleton, not shared across Node.js replicas.
CACHE_TTL_MS = 5 * 60 * 1000 — stale entries survive 5 minutes.

add() performs integer addition on cents — safe, but no property test verifies commutativity or associativity across arbitrary amounts.
multiply() uses Math.round(cents * factor) — for factors like 1/3 this introduces accumulation error that is untested over chains of multiplications.

findings.title: ZepMemoryAdapter.initialize() is not idempotent under concurrent callers — two simultaneous first calls both proceed past the isInitializedFlag guard

findings.title: assignRole two-step lookup-then-upsert: concurrent revoke interleaves between findUnique and upsert
findings.businessRule: Role assignment must be atomic: if a revocation completes between the role-lookup and the upsert, the upsert must not re-create the assignment.

findings.title: In-process permission cache is not invalidated across concurrent instances sharing the module singleton
findings.businessRule: After a role is assigned or revoked, the effective permission set for the user must not be served from a stale cache beyond a bounded window.
