evidence.note: performAccountReassign reads existing.ownerId OUTSIDE the transaction. This value is returned as previousOwnerId in the OK verdict.
The inner $transaction only does user verification + updateMany. It does not re-read the current ownerId, so previousOwnerId in the verdict is the stale outer-read value.

emitAccountReassignSideEffects fires audit log with beforeState: { ownerId: previousOwnerId } — this will be wrong if a concurrent reassign changed the owner between the outer read and the inner transaction commit.
Identical pattern in performContactReassign: outer findFirst, SKIPPED check, then inner $transaction with updateMany but no re-read of ownerId.
Identical pattern in performDealReassign.

findings.existingMitigations: updateMany with tenant-scoped WHERE { id, tenantId } ensures the update is applied to the correct record. If count===0 the caller returns NOT_FOUND. The actual ownership write is correctly serialized. Only the audit previousOwnerId and the SKIPPED idempotency check are vulnerable to stale reads.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none needed — fix is to move the ownerId capture inside the $transaction (read current ownerId inside tx, return it from tx as previousOwnerId)
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ entityId: fc.uuid(), ownerA: fc.uuid(), ownerB: fc.uuid(), ownerC: fc.uuid() }) — T1 reassigns entity to ownerB, T2 concurrently reassigns to ownerC; assert audit.previousOwnerId matches actual DB ownerId at time of T2 commit
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: findFirst(entity) -> T2: findFirst(entity) [both see ownerId=A] -> T1: $tx updateMany(ownerId=B) commits -> T2: $tx updateMany(ownerId=C) commits — T2 audit says previousOwnerId=A but actual was B
fixStrategyCandidates: Inside the $transaction in performAccountReassign, add a tx.account.findFirst to re-read current ownerId; use that as previousOwnerId in the returned verdict
Alternatively use tx.account.update (not updateMany) with WHERE { id, tenantId } and SELECT previousOwnerId via a raw UPDATE ... RETURNING statement
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed, but severity downgraded from High to Medium. The ownership write itself is correctly serialized: tx.account.updateMany({ where: { id, tenantId }, data: { ownerId } }) at account-reassign.ts:80-84 ensures the correct new owner is written atomically, and count===0 returns NOT_FOUND. What is wrong: existing.ownerId is captured at account-reassign.ts:48-51 OUTSIDE the $transaction, and returned as previousOwnerId at line 87-89 from the outer closure. The $transaction at lines 73-92 never re-reads ownerId. Two concurrent reassigns T1 and T2 both read ownerId=A at the outer findFirst; T1 commits (ownerId→B); T2 commits (ownerId→C) — T2's audit says previousOwnerId=A when the actual previous was B. The same pattern is confirmed in contact-reassign.ts:35-77 and deal-reassign.ts:36-77. Impact is limited to audit log fidelity and notification targeting, not the ownership record itself. Medium is appropriate. Account, Contact, and Opportunity models all have ownerId as non-nullable String with no idempotency constraint that would prevent this.

findings.title: routing.reorder: count-then-transaction TOCTOU allows cross-tenant priority mutation
findings.businessRule: A tenant can only reorder routing rules that belong to their own tenant; a rule transferred or deleted between the count check and the transaction cannot be mutated.
invalidStates: Rule deleted between count check and transaction, causing $transaction to throw P2025 (record not found) — currently unhandled, will surface as 500
Rule transferred to different tenant between check and transaction — update proceeds on a rule the caller no longer owns

evidence.note: routingRule.count(WHERE id IN ruleIds AND tenantId) runs OUTSIDE the subsequent $transaction. Ownership is verified by count match.
The $transaction only does .update({ where: { id } }) — no tenantId guard in the WHERE. If a rule is reassigned to another tenant between the count and the transaction, the update proceeds.
@@unique([tenantId, name]) on RoutingRule guarantees name uniqueness per tenant but does not prevent rule re-parenting in an adversarial scenario.

findings.existingMitigations: The @@unique([tenantId, name]) constraint prevents duplicate names; multi-tenant isolation in tRPC context limits realistic attack surface. Rule transfer between tenants has no UI path in the current codebase, making this a recoverable medium-severity risk.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — fix is code: move the ownership count check inside the $transaction, or add tenantId to each individual update WHERE clause
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.uuid(), { minLength: 1, maxLength: 20 }) as ruleIds — simulate concurrent delete of one ruleId between count and transaction
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: count(ruleIds, tenantId) = N (PASS) -> T2: delete(ruleIds[0]) -> T1: $transaction update(ruleIds[0]) -> P2025 or wrong-tenant write
fixStrategyCandidates: Add tenantId to each update WHERE clause inside the $transaction: update({ where: { id: rule.id, tenantId } })
Wrap count check and all updates in a single $transaction with SERIALIZABLE isolation
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. In routing.router.ts:163-165 the ownership count check runs outside the subsequent $transaction. The $transaction at lines 173-180 uses the array form (batch transaction) and each update is { where: { id: rule.id } } with NO tenantId guard. The createTenantScopedPrisma implementation (tenant-context.ts:119-163) sets app.current_tenant_id via a session variable for RLS but explicitly warns (tenant-context.ts:114-117) that a different PgBouncer connection landing on a later query would see the previous session value — meaning RLS is not a reliable defense for mid-request connection changes. A concurrent delete of any ruleId between the count check and the transaction commit results in a Prisma P2025 (record not found) that is unhandled by the caller, surfacing as a 500. RoutingRule.@@unique([tenantId, name]) (schema.prisma:3796) prevents name collisions but does not prevent this TOCTOU. Cross-tenant mutation is theoretical (no rule-transfer code path exists), making the unhandled P2025 the primary real risk.

findings.title: LeadRoutingService: forceReroute races with concurrent ordinary routeLead — double capacity increment
findings.businessRule: When forceReroute reassigns an already-owned lead, the previous agent's capacity is decremented and the new agent's capacity is incremented exactly once; capacity does not drift after any number of reassigns.
invalidStates: agentA.currentCapacity remains inflated after its lead is force-rerouted to agentB — agentA appears over-loaded and may be excluded from future routing even though it has capacity
Both concurrent routeLead (ordinary) and forceReroute routing calls succeed on the same lead — lead.ownerId correct (last-write-wins) but agentAvailability incremented twice with no decrement

evidence.note: The idempotency guard throws if ownerId is set and forceReroute is false. With forceReroute=true, the guard is bypassed and the transaction proceeds unconditionally.

Step 2 updates lead.ownerId and Step 3 increments the NEW agent's capacity, but there is NO decrement of the PREVIOUS agent's capacity when forceReroute is used. If lead.ownerId was already set to agentA, agentA's currentCapacity is never decremented.
The transaction reads existing ownerId from the lead (line 256 references lead.ownerId) but does not decrement agentAvailability for the previous owner.

findings.existingMitigations: The guard at line 256 prevents ordinary double-routing; the $transaction wraps the write. The missing piece is the capacity decrement for the replaced agent.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — fix is code: inside the forceReroute path, read the current ownerId inside the transaction and decrement that agent's currentCapacity before incrementing the new agent's
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ leadId: fc.uuid(), agentA: fc.uuid(), agentB: fc.uuid() }) — T1 runs ordinary routeLead (lead.ownerId=null), T2 runs forceReroute concurrently; assert each agent's currentCapacity changed by exactly +1 or -1 as expected
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: routeLead (no forceReroute, lead.ownerId=null) -> T2: routeLead (forceReroute=true, same lead) -> T1 commits (ownerId=A, A.capacity++) -> T2 commits (ownerId=B, B.capacity++, A.capacity NOT decremented)
fixStrategyCandidates: Inside _routeLeadImpl $transaction, after reading lead.ownerId, if it is set and forceReroute is true, add tx.agentAvailability.updateMany({ where: { userId: lead.ownerId }, data: { currentCapacity: { decrement: 1 } } }) before the increment for the new agent
Combine both the decrement of old agent and increment of new agent in a single SQL UPDATE CASE expression via $executeRaw for atomicity
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. In LeadRoutingService.ts:336-349, when forceReroute=true and lead.ownerId is already set to agentA, the $transaction updates lead.ownerId to agentB and increments agentB's currentCapacity (line 346-349), but has no corresponding decrement of agentA's currentCapacity. The idempotency guard at line 256 only fires when forceReroute is false; bypassing it does not trigger any cleanup of the previous owner's capacity slot. The result is agentA.currentCapacity remains permanently inflated after every force-reroute. This is a logic defect that exists even in single-threaded execution — the concurrency framing (two concurrent calls) amplifies the effect but the bug is present without concurrency. Severity Medium (not High) because capacity drift is recoverable (manual reset, periodic recalculation) and does not corrupt monetary or auth state.

findings.title: routing.toggle and routing.delete: findFirst ownership check outside transaction; update/delete WHERE has no tenantId
findings.businessRule: A tenant can only toggle or delete routing rules that belong to their own tenant; a concurrent delete between the ownership check and the write must not result in an unhandled 500 or cross-tenant mutation.
invalidStates: Concurrent delete of the same rule by two callers: second delete throws P2025, surfaces as unhandled 500
Rule deleted between findFirst and toggle update: P2025 unhandled 500 instead of NOT_FOUND

evidence.note: toggle: routingRule.findFirst({ where: { id, tenantId } }) runs outside any transaction. The subsequent update({ where: { id: input.id } }) has no tenantId guard. A concurrent delete of the rule between the findFirst and the update throws Prisma P2025, unhandled.
delete: same pattern — findFirst with tenantId, then delete({ where: { id: input.id } }) without tenantId. Concurrent double-delete throws P2025 unhandled.

RLS via SET app.current_tenant_id is not guaranteed when PgBouncer hands a different connection to a later query in the same request; application-layer tenantId WHERE is the reliable guard. toggle/delete omit it.

findings.existingMitigations: RLS session variable (tenant-context.ts:154-156) provides partial DB-level protection but the code comment explicitly documents the connection-reuse risk
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — code fix only
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ ruleId: fc.uuid(), tenantId: fc.uuid() }) — concurrent toggle and delete targeting the same ruleId
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: findFirst(rule, tenantId=X) PASS -> T2: delete(rule) commits -> T1: update({ where: { id } }) -> P2025 unhandled
T1: findFirst(rule) PASS -> T2: findFirst(rule) PASS -> T1: delete commits -> T2: delete throws P2025
fixStrategyCandidates: Add tenantId to the update/delete WHERE clause: update({ where: { id: input.id, tenantId } }) — Prisma will return count=0 instead of throwing P2025
Wrap findFirst + update/delete in a single $transaction for each endpoint
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: bulkReassign sequential loop: concurrent bulk operations targeting overlapping account sets produce stale-previousOwnerId audit entries at scale
findings.businessRule: Each audit entry in a bulk reassign records the actual owner at the time the individual row write committed, not a snapshot taken at loop-start.
invalidStates: Audit rows for 50-account bulk reassign record previousOwnerId values that were already superseded by a concurrent single-item reassign that raced between the outer read and the tx commit
Notifications sent to the wrong previous-owner for rows where a race changed ownership in the gap

evidence.note: bulkReassign iterates account IDs in a sequential for-loop. Each iteration calls performAccountReassign which does an outer findFirst (outside $transaction) then a $transaction. Under two concurrent bulkReassign calls on overlapping IDs, the outer read for a given accountId in Call-2 may execute after Call-1's $transaction has committed, giving Call-2 a correct previousOwnerId — OR before, giving a stale one. The race window is the same as RACE-ROUTI-03 but repeated N times per bulk call without any cross-iteration locking.

Target user is pre-validated once before the loop. A TOCTOU on user deletion between the pre-check and any per-row tx is acknowledged in the code comment (line 1069-1073) but the audit previousOwnerId stale-read is not addressed.

performAccountReassign captures existing.ownerId at line 48-51 outside the $transaction and returns it as previousOwnerId at line 87 — the same gap as RACE-ROUTI-03, applied per-row inside the bulk loop.

findings.existingMitigations: tx.account.updateMany WHERE { id, tenantId } in account-reassign.ts:80-84 serializes the ownership write per row; count===0 guard prevents ghost writes
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — code fix only
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.uuid(), { minLength: 5, maxLength: 20 }) as accountIds — two concurrent bulkReassign calls on overlapping subsets; assert each audit previousOwnerId matches the actual DB ownerId at commit time
commandSequenceCandidates: BulkCall-1 outer-read(acct[3]) sees ownerId=A -> BulkCall-2 tx(acct[3]) commits ownerId=C -> BulkCall-1 tx(acct[3]) commits ownerId=B, audit says previousOwnerId=A (actual was C)
fixStrategyCandidates: Move the ownerId capture inside the $transaction in performAccountReassign: add tx.account.findFirst inside the transaction and use its ownerId as previousOwnerId
Use UPDATE ... RETURNING to atomically capture previousOwnerId via $executeRaw
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: Integration test project runs up to 4 fork workers sharing one global testcontainer — concurrent tests can corrupt shared DB state without per-test isolation
findings.businessRule: Each integration test must run against isolated DB state; with maxWorkers=4, multiple test files execute concurrently against the same PostgreSQL container with no per-file transaction or schema isolation.
invalidStates: Worker A's beforeEach TRUNCATE races with Worker B's INSERT — FK violation aborts B's test
Two workers both INSERT a unique-keyed record (e.g. same tenantId) — one silently wins, the other gets a P2002 Prisma error unrelated to its actual test logic

evidence.note: Integration project: pool='forks', maxWorkers=4 — up to 4 test files run concurrently in separate fork processes.
startPostgresContainer() starts a single PostgreSQL container. databaseUrl is set once globally — all 4 fork workers share the same DATABASE_URL and thus the same database.

resetDatabaseState() performs TRUNCATE CASCADE on all tables. If two workers call this concurrently (each in beforeEach), they race: worker A truncates mid-insert by worker B, producing FK violations or empty-result false-passes.

findings.existingMitigations: tests/integration/db.test.ts (the main db test file) was stripped of all real assertions on 2026-04-17 and replaced with a single env-guard test. Currently there are very few integration tests that actually write to the DB (graceful-skip pattern limits real-DB runs), so the race is latent. No schema-per-worker or savepoint isolation exists.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none
findings.generatedInputs: N/A — infrastructure concurrency gap, not a domain property test
fixStrategyCandidates: Set maxWorkers=1 for the integration project (sequential execution, no shared-state race) — simplest fix
Create a per-worker Postgres schema (CREATE SCHEMA worker_N) in beforeAll and set search_path; drop in afterAll
Use testcontainers perWorkerSetup hook to spin one container per Vitest fork worker
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: scripts/run-tests.js parses only the first 'Tests X passed' line — multi-project runs with partial failures produce a misleading zero-failed exit
findings.businessRule: The pnpm test script must surface ALL test failures to CI; if any project reports failures that are overwritten by a later 'X passed' stdout line, CI can exit 0 while tests are red.
invalidStates: run-tests.js reports '0 failed' in its summary while vitest exited 1 — developers see contradictory signals
The parsed testsPassedCount reflects only the last vitest summary line, not the total across all 17 projects

evidence.note: stdout handler assigns testsPassedCount = parseInt(testsMatch[1]) and testsFailedCount = parseInt(failedMatch[1]) on each chunk. For a 17-project run, vitest emits multiple summary lines — the last 'Tests X passed' line (from the final project) overwrites counts from earlier projects. A project that failed early in the run can be hidden if the final project passes.

findings.existingMitigations: vitest itself returns a non-zero exit code when any test fails, and the `close` handler at line 73 propagates that exit code — so CI will catch failures at the process level. However, the parsed count-based pass/fail reporting shown in CI step summaries and the run-tests.js console output can misreport the failure count, causing confusing output and potentially masking which project failed.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none
findings.generatedInputs: N/A — CI reporting gap, not a domain property test
fixStrategyCandidates: Switch to accumulating counts: testsPassedCount += parseInt(match[1]) and testsFailedCount += parseInt(match[1]) instead of assignment
Parse only the final aggregate summary line by matching the 'Test Files' line emitted once at the end of all projects
Remove the custom parser entirely and rely on vitest's --reporter=json for structured exit-code-plus-counts output
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: WebhookFramework.IdempotencyStore: in-memory check-then-set is not atomic under concurrent async invocations
findings.businessRule: A webhook event delivered twice (same event.id from same source) must execute handlers exactly once per framework instance.
invalidStates: Two concurrent handle() calls with the same (sourceName, event.id) both pass the has() check before either writes via set(); both execute all handlers; business side-effects are doubled.

evidence.note: idempotency.has(idempotencyKey) check at line 612 returns false for a key not yet set. The key is only written at line 659 after all handlers complete. If two concurrent handle() calls arrive with the same event.id before either completes, both pass the has() check.
IdempotencyStore is a plain Map with no mutex or atomic CAS. has() and set() are separate method calls on lines 264-270 and 279-281 respectively.

idempotency.set() is called only AFTER await executeWithMiddleware completes (line 654-656). The window between the has() check at line 612 and the set() at line 659 spans an entire async handler invocation, which may yield to other microtasks.

Idempotency test at line 116 sends two sequential awaited calls — this does NOT exercise simultaneous async interleaving because Node.js is single-threaded but the gap between has() and set() is an async yield point.

findings.existingMitigations: Node.js single-threaded event loop means true CPU parallelism is impossible, but two Promise.all-driven concurrent handle() calls can still interleave at every await point between has() (line 612) and set() (line 659). The window is the full handler execution duration.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: None — in-memory store; fix is to set the key to a Promise sentinel immediately on first check (pre-mark pattern) and short-circuit concurrent callers that see the pending sentinel.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ sourceName: fc.string(), eventId: fc.string(), concurrency: fc.integer({ min: 2, max: 8 }) }) — use Promise.all to fire N simultaneous handle() calls with the same eventId; count handler invocations and assert === 1.
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: has(key)=false → T2: has(key)=false → T1: executeHandlers → T2: executeHandlers → T1: set(key) → T2: set(key)
fixStrategyCandidates: Change IdempotencyStore to store a pending Promise: on first call, insert a Promise before dispatching and await it; concurrent callers await the same Promise and receive the cached result.
Use a simple in-memory Set as a 'claiming' guard: add the key synchronously before the first await, remove on error; callers that find the key already present return Duplicate immediately.
For multi-instance deployments: replace IdempotencyStore with a Redis SET NX with TTL so the check-then-set is atomic across processes.
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. IdempotencyStore (packages/webhooks/src/framework.ts:256-293) is a plain Map with separate has() and set() calls. handle() checks idempotency.has(key) at line 612 and writes idempotency.set(key) only at line 659 after awaiting executeWithMiddleware, which runs Promise.all(handlers). The window between has() and set() spans the entire handler execution, which contains multiple await points. Two concurrent handle() calls with the same eventId both observe has()=false before either completes and both proceed to execute handlers. The idempotency test at framework.test.ts:116-125 uses two sequential awaited calls — this cannot exercise the concurrent interleaving because the second call only starts after the first is fully resolved. Downgraded from the auditor's proposed Medium — severity is already Medium, confirmed as correct given in-memory, single-process scope.

findings.title: OutboxEventBusAdapter.publishAll: withTransaction uses module-level singleton prisma, not injected this.prisma
findings.businessRule: publishAll must write all events atomically using the same database connection/transaction as the calling domain service to guarantee the outbox pattern.
invalidStates: Consumer passes a tenant-specific PrismaClient as `prisma` option; publishAll silently writes to the singleton's DB connection instead; events are written to the wrong tenant's connection or a different isolation context.
Integration test creates a PrismaClient pointing to a test DB; publishAll writes to the application DB because withTransaction uses the singleton.

evidence.note: import { withTransaction } from '@intelliflow/db' — this function wraps the module-level singleton `prisma` client (packages/db/src/client.ts:173-179), not the `this.prisma` instance injected at construction time.
publishAll calls withTransaction(...) which internally calls `(prisma as any).$transaction(fn)` — the singleton, regardless of which PrismaClient was injected.
withTransaction is hardcoded to the module-level singleton `prisma`, not a parameterised client.

Test mocks @intelliflow/db entirely including withTransaction (line 16-25), so tests pass regardless of which prisma client is used — the connection mismatch is not observable in the mock environment.

findings.existingMitigations: In production with a single global Prisma singleton the behaviour is correct. The risk manifests in tests (injecting a per-test client bypasses the mock) and multi-tenant scenarios where separate PrismaClient instances with different connection strings are injected.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: None — pure code-architecture fix.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ tenantId: fc.uuid() }) — property: for any injected prisma client, the transaction in publishAll must run on that same client, not an external singleton.
commandSequenceCandidates: Inject test PrismaClient A → call publishAll → observe transaction runs on singleton client B
fixStrategyCandidates: Replace `withTransaction(fn)` with `this.prisma.$transaction(fn)` in publishAll to honour the injected client.
Add a `withTransaction` overload that accepts a PrismaClient parameter and use it here.
Pass a `transactionFn` as a constructor option to OutboxEventBusAdapter for full injection control.
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. packages/db/src/client.ts:178-184 shows withTransaction is hardcoded to the module-level singleton `prisma`: `return (prisma as any).$transaction(fn)` — no client parameter. OutboxEventBusAdapter.ts:13 imports this function, and publishAll at line 115 calls `await withTransaction(...)` without passing this.prisma. Any consumer injecting a tenant-specific or test-specific PrismaClient will have publishAll silently use the singleton's connection instead. The test at OutboxEventBusAdapter.impl.test.ts:15-25 mocks @intelliflow/db entirely including withTransaction, so the connection mismatch is invisible in tests. Severity is Medium (not Critical) because in the current production deployment there is a single global singleton, so the practical risk is primarily in multi-tenant injection or test isolation breakage rather than live data corruption today.

findings.title: WebhookFramework.reprocessDeadLetter: bypasses idempotency store, allowing re-duplicate on concurrent reprocess calls
findings.businessRule: Manually reprocessing a dead-letter event must not produce duplicate handler effects if called concurrently or if the event was already successfully handled.
invalidStates: Two concurrent reprocessDeadLetter('eventId') calls both find the entry, both call deadLetterQueue.remove(), both execute handlers — doubling side-effects.

evidence.note: reprocessDeadLetter removes the entry from DLQ at line 803 then calls Promise.all(handlers) at line 812 but never calls idempotency.set(). If reprocessing succeeds, the same eventId can be handled again via the normal handle() path (which checks idempotency) but another concurrent reprocessDeadLetter call sees no idempotency entry and re-executes handlers.

DLQ entry is removed before handlers succeed (line 803 precedes line 812). A concurrent second call finds the entry missing (returns false) but a race between two simultaneous reprocess calls on the same eventId can both find the entry and both execute handlers.

findings.existingMitigations: If two calls arrive sequentially the second finds entries.find() returns undefined and returns false (no double execution). The race only occurs if both calls hit the find() before either remove() executes — possible under concurrent async invocations.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: None — in-memory fix.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ eventId: fc.string() }) — schedule two simultaneous reprocessDeadLetter calls with the same eventId; assert handler called exactly once.
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: find(eventId) found → T2: find(eventId) found → T1: remove → T2: remove → T1: handlers → T2: handlers
fixStrategyCandidates: Remove the entry from DLQ atomically before any await by using a synchronous splice, then check if removal succeeded (splice returns the removed item) before executing handlers.
Call idempotency.set(key, ...) after successful reprocessing to prevent normal handle() path from re-executing.
Use the same pre-mark sentinel pattern as RACE-WEBHO-03 fix.
findings.verificationReason: Confirmed. reprocessDeadLetter (packages/webhooks/src/framework.ts:798-817) calls deadLetterQueue.getAll() which returns a shallow copy of the entries array (DeadLetterQueue.getAll at line 377-379 returns [...this.entries]). Two concurrent calls both call getAll() and find() on their own copies before either calls remove() on the backing this.entries. Both pass the entry !== null check at line 801, both call deadLetterQueue.remove() (the second remove returns false but does not abort execution), and both proceed to execute handlers. The auditor's description is correct. Severity stays Medium: in-memory, no durable state or financial impact, limited to the webhook framework's DLQ path.

findings.title: WebhookFramework.processRetries: retry path sets idempotency.set() after handler success but does not check idempotency.has() first — concurrent retry and handle() calls can double-execute
findings.businessRule: A retried webhook event whose idempotency key is already marked as processed must not be executed again by the normal handle() path or a concurrent retry.
invalidStates: Event is in retryQueue. handle() is called concurrently with processRetries(). processRetries() begins executing handlers without checking has(). handle() passes has()=false (not yet set) and also executes handlers. Both complete and both set idempotency.set(). Handler side-effects duplicated.

evidence.note: processRetries() calls executeWithMiddleware and on success calls idempotency.set(key, ...) at line 758. However processRetries() never calls idempotency.has(key) before executing handlers. If handle() is called for the same event concurrently with processRetries(), the retry path bypasses the idempotency check that handle() at line 612 would normally perform.
processRetries() has no idempotency.has() guard at entry. The same event can simultaneously be running through handle() (which does check has()) and through processRetries() (which does not).

findings.existingMitigations: handle() checks idempotency.has() at line 612 before executing, but processRetries() does not. Retry entries are removed from retryQueue via getReady() at the start of processRetries (retryQueue removes ready entries during getReady call at line 341), so two processRetries() calls won't double-process the same entry — the risk is specifically a race between the retry path and a new handle() call for the same event.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: None — in-memory fix.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ eventId: fc.string(), sourceName: fc.string() }) — schedule simultaneous processRetries() and handle() with the same eventId; count total handler invocations and assert === 1.
commandSequenceCandidates: T1: processRetries → getReady (entry removed from queue) → executeHandlers (no has() check) → T2: handle → has(key)=false (not yet set) → executeHandlers → T1: set(key) → T2: set(key)
fixStrategyCandidates: Add idempotency.has(key) check at the start of the retry execution loop in processRetries(), returning early if the key is already marked.
Use the same pre-mark sentinel pattern: synchronously insert the key into idempotency before the first await in both handle() and processRetries().
findings.verificationReason: surfaced by adversarial verifier

findings.title: MaintenanceScheduler stale-lead/task/deal/appointment: findFirst-then-create dedup gap produces duplicate reminder notifications
findings.businessRule: A stale-lead, overdue-task, stale-deal, or appointment-reminder notification must be sent at most once per dedup window regardless of concurrent scheduler instances.
invalidStates: Recipient receives multiple identical stale-lead reminder emails/notifications within the dedup window
Appointment organizer receives N copies of the 15-minute reminder

evidence.note: checkFollowUpReminders: for each stale lead, calls notification.findFirst to check for a recent reminder, then notification.create. The read and write are not in a transaction. Two concurrent runs both see no recent reminder and both insert.
Same pattern for overdue tasks (sourceType='overdue_task_reminder').
Same pattern for stale deals (sourceType='stale_deal_alert', 48h window).
Same pattern for appointment reminders (sourceType='appointment_reminder', 1h window). The 5-min interval makes overlap especially likely.
No unique constraint on (tenantId, sourceId, sourceType, createdAt window); @@index([tenantId, recipientId, sourceType, createdAt]) is only an index, not a constraint.

findings.existingMitigations: Application-level window check via findFirst with createdAt >= now-24h. Not atomic; races exist in the gap between read and write.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: Partial unique index: @@unique([tenantId, sourceId, sourceType]) WHERE status IN ('PENDING','SENT','DELIVERED') — or use upsert with an ignored conflict key

findings.title: Email.create normalisation is idempotent but ValueObject.equals uses JSON.stringify which does not handle nested Date objects in props consistently
findings.businessRule: ValueObject equality must be reflexive (v.equals(v)===true) and symmetric (v.equals(w)===w.equals(v)) for all valid instances.
invalidStates: Two TimeSlot instances holding dates differing only in milliseconds (e.g. from setSystemTime) may or may not be equal depending on how the dates were constructed

evidence.note: equals() uses JSON.stringify(this.props) === JSON.stringify(other.props). JSON.stringify of a Date produces a quoted ISO string, so two TimeSlot instances holding equivalent Date objects will compare correctly. However, if a props field holds a non-serialisable value (undefined, function, Symbol) JSON.stringify silently omits it, making two structurally-different objects appear equal.

TimeSlot.props contains Date objects — these serialize to strings via JSON.stringify, which is correct. But a TimeSlot reconstituted from a string date vs a real Date object would produce different JSON if one side has milliseconds truncated.

findings.title: OutboxEventBusAdapter.publish: single-event path lacks transaction, violating outbox atomicity guarantee
findings.businessRule: An outbox event must be written in the same transaction as the domain aggregate state change that triggered it — a crash between aggregate save and event write must not produce a ghost event.
invalidStates: findFirst returns null (no duplicate) → DB error between findFirst and create → event is not written but caller received no error (if error is swallowed upstream) → event lost.
Concurrent callers both pass findFirst (RACE-WEBHO-01) → both write duplicate rows.

evidence.note: publish() executes findFirst (line 66-78) and create (line 83-102) as two independent Prisma calls with no $transaction wrapper. publishAll wraps in withTransaction at line 115, but the single-event path does not.
publishAll correctly uses withTransaction for the batch path, making the inconsistency between single and batch paths visible.

findings.existingMitigations: The true outbox atomicity guarantee (aggregate save + event write in one transaction) is the caller's responsibility — the publish() method alone cannot enforce it. However the absence of even an internal findFirst+create transaction means the idempotency check in publish() has a TOCTOU gap (see RACE-WEBHO-01) and any error between the two calls can leave the store in an inconsistent state.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: As per RACE-WEBHO-01: unique index on (tenantId, idempotencyKey) would make the create fail on duplicate and remove the need for a pre-check entirely.
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ eventType: fc.string(), aggregateId: fc.uuid() }) — property: for any event, after publish() completes without throwing, exactly one row with the matching idempotencyKey exists in the outbox.
commandSequenceCandidates: findFirst (no duplicate) → DB write fails → caller sees error → no row written (benign but event lost if not retried)
findFirst (no duplicate) → create succeeds → concurrent findFirst (reads committed: still no dup) → concurrent create (duplicate row)
fixStrategyCandidates: Wrap findFirst+create in this.prisma.$transaction() for the single-event publish() path, mirroring publishAll.
Add a DB-level unique constraint and switch to upsert/createMany({skipDuplicates:true}), removing the application-level duplicate check entirely.
findings.verificationReason: The outbox-atomicity framing (aggregate save + event write in one transaction) is a caller responsibility, and publishAll correctly uses withTransaction at line 115. The single publish() path has no internal $transaction, but a caller managing their own outer $transaction would include publish() within it. The real independent risk is the TOCTOU gap between findFirst and create within publish() — which is already captured and rated High in RACE-WEBHO-01. This finding adds no new race not already covered: the 'ghost event' scenario requires a crash between two calls that are both internal to publish() with no outer transaction, which is the identical TOCTOU as RACE-WEBHO-01. Downgraded to Low to avoid double-counting; the unique aspect (outbox atomicity caller contract) is a design note, not an independently exploitable race. Evidence: packages/adapters/src/events/OutboxEventBusAdapter.ts:62-103 and packages/adapters/src/events/OutboxEventBusAdapter.ts:110-158.

Same lead scored twice in rapid succession, second write overwrites first with stale LLM output

evidence.note: handleScheduledDispatch: builds a new Queue instance, iterates tenantsWithUnscoredLeads, and calls queue.add('score-lead', ...) for every lead without a jobId dedup key. If two AI worker instances both consume the same sentinel __scheduled__ job (BullMQ locks prevent this within one queue, but a restart race or lock expiry via stalled job can deliver it twice), duplicate per-lead jobs are enqueued.
dispatchScheduledInsights: same pattern — queue.add('generate-insights', payload) without a jobId or deduplicationId. No per-tenant dedup key passed to BullMQ.

upsertJobScheduler is used, which BullMQ guarantees deduplicates the repeatable trigger. However the sentinel job itself has attempts:3 (DEFAULT_INSIGHT_JOB_OPTIONS), so a transient failure + retry causes the fan-out loop to run again and re-enqueue all leads.

findings.existingMitigations: upsertJobScheduler in ai-worker.ts deduplicates the sentinel's schedule entry. The downstream upsert on LeadAIInsight (@@unique([leadId, tenantId])) means the DB write is idempotent even if the job runs twice. BullMQ worker locking prevents the same sentinel job from being picked up by two workers simultaneously under normal conditions.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none additional — upsert is already idempotent at DB level
findings.generatedInputs: fc.array(fc.uuid(), { minLength: 1, maxLength: 20 }) for leadIds; simulate sentinel job running twice with shared Queue mock; assert queue.add was called with each leadId exactly once
commandSequenceCandidates: Sentinel job picked by worker-1, handleScheduledDispatch enqueues lead-A..lead-Z; sentinel job stalls (lock expires); sentinel retried by worker-2 → lead-A..lead-Z enqueued again
fixStrategyCandidates: Pass a deterministic jobId to queue.add: { jobId: `score-lead-${leadId}-${tenantId}-${dayBucket}` } — BullMQ deduplicates by jobId within the same queue
Set attempts:1 on the sentinel job so a failure does not re-trigger the fan-out loop; move the retry budget to per-lead jobs
findings.verificationReason: apps/ai-worker/src/jobs/scoring.job.ts:330-346 — queue.add('score-lead', ..., DEFAULT_SCORING_JOB_OPTIONS) is called without a jobId dedup key; DEFAULT_SCORING_JOB_OPTIONS has attempts:3 (confirmed at line 534), so a transient sentinel failure causes the fan-out to re-enqueue all leads. However, the DB write at scoring.job.ts:399 uses prisma.leadAIInsight.upsert keyed on @@unique([leadId, tenantId]) (schema.prisma:2707), making the final stored result idempotent. The impact is redundant LLM calls and queue bloat, not data corruption. Downgraded from Medium to Low because the DB layer absorbs the duplicate-write hazard.

findings.title: insight-generation.job: persistEntityActivity uses findFirst-then-create without unique constraint — duplicate LeadActivity/ContactActivity rows on parallel insight jobs
findings.businessRule: Each AI insight must produce at most one activity feed entry per (leadId/contactId, title, tenantId) combination.
invalidStates: Lead activity feed shows duplicate '[AI] Hot lead' entries
Contact timeline shows duplicate AI insight entries with identical content

evidence.note: persistEntityActivity: calls prisma.leadActivity.findFirst({ where: { leadId, title, tenantId } }) then prisma.leadActivity.create. Same for contactActivity. No transaction wraps both. Concurrent jobs for the same tenant can both see null and both insert.
LeadActivity model has no @@unique constraint on (leadId, title, tenantId). Only indexes exist.
ContactActivity model similarly has no @@unique constraint on (contactId, title, tenantId).

findings.existingMitigations: Application-level findFirst dedup check is present but not atomic.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: @@unique([leadId, title, tenantId]) on LeadActivity (partial: WHERE type='SCORE_UPDATE'), similar for ContactActivity
findings.generatedInputs: fc.record({ leadId: fc.uuid(), tenantId: fc.uuid(), title: fc.string() }); run two concurrent persistEntityActivity calls; assert LeadActivity count for (leadId, title) === 1
commandSequenceCandidates: Job-A: leadActivity.findFirst({leadId, title}) → null; Job-B: same → null; Job-A: leadActivity.create; Job-B: leadActivity.create → 2 rows
fixStrategyCandidates: Add @@unique([leadId, title, tenantId]) and use createOrSkip via raw INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING
Wrap findFirst+create in a $transaction with isolation SERIALIZABLE
findings.verificationReason: apps/ai-worker/src/jobs/insight-generation.job.ts:692-728 — leadActivity.findFirst then leadActivity.create (and contactActivity equivalent) are not in a transaction. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:487-508 (LeadActivity) and 1239-1272 (ContactActivity) have no @@unique constraint on (leadId, title, tenantId) or (contactId, title, tenantId); only indexes exist. Concurrent insight jobs for the same tenant can both see null and both create duplicate activity feed entries. Severity Low is appropriate: activity entries are informational/append-only, duplicates are visible but not financially harmful and are recoverable.

findings.title: RetryBudgetTracker singleton: canRetry/consumeRetry pair has a TOCTOU gap under concurrent async callers in the same process
findings.businessRule: The in-process retry budget must not allow more enqueues than the configured defaultBudget within one windowMs, even when multiple async callers race through canRetry+consumeRetry.
invalidStates: Up to (concurrency factor) * defaultBudget enqueues succeed in one window instead of exactly defaultBudget

evidence.note: canRetry reads budget.remaining > 0 (line 189); consumeRetry later decrements budget.remaining-- (line 202). In Node.js single-threaded event loop these two are safe when called synchronously, BUT if canRetry is checked, then an await occurs, then consumeRetry is called, the budget may have been consumed by a concurrent coroutine in the interim. The primary defect (RACE-WORKE-04) means consumeRetry is never called, but if fixed, this gap becomes relevant.
enqueueAIScoring is async. After canRetry() (sync), the function does several awaits before the budget is consumed. Any concurrent caller can pass canRetry() before any consume happens.

findings.existingMitigations: Node.js event loop is single-threaded for synchronous code; canRetry and consumeRetry are both synchronous. The gap only manifests if an await is placed between them — which enqueueAIScoring does.
findings.requiredDbConstraints: none — in-process only
findings.generatedInputs: fc.integer({ min: 2, max: 20 }) for concurrent caller count; assert sum of successful enqueues <= defaultBudget after Promise.all(N x enqueueAIScoring)
commandSequenceCandidates: 10 concurrent callers each call canRetry() → all see remaining=100 → all await queue.add → all call consumeRetry() → remaining decremented 10x from 100 to 90, but all 10 succeeded (correct if sequential, but budget not atomically checked-and-decremented)
fixStrategyCandidates: Merge canRetry + consumeRetry into a single synchronous tryConsume() method that atomically reads-and-decrements in one call, called immediately before the await queue.add
findings.verificationReason: packages/platform/src/queues/retry-strategy.ts:187-203 — canRetry and consumeRetry are both synchronous. In the Node.js single-threaded event loop, concurrent async coroutines can only interleave at await boundaries. packages/platform/src/queues/queue-factory.ts:226-256 does place awaits between the canRetry check (line 226) and any point where consumeRetry would be called. However, since RACE-WORKE-04 establishes that consumeRetry is NEVER called in this path, this TOCTOU window is currently moot — it only becomes exploitable once RACE-WORKE-04 is fixed. A targeted test (Promise.all of N concurrent enqueueAIScoring calls after fixing RACE-WORKE-04) would settle whether the event-loop interleaving produces over-budget enqueues in practice.

dismissedFalsePositives.title: Calendar delete: ownership check and delete transaction are not atomic — TOCTOU on owner field
dismissedFalsePositives.reason: The auditor's attack vector requires a concurrent ownership transfer (update(id, {ownerId: B})) to race against the delete handler. packages/validators/src/calendar.ts lines 10-17: updateCalendarSchema accepts only { id, name?, color? } — ownerId is not a field. apps/api/src/modules/calendar/calendar.router.ts lines 79-92: the update mutation's data spread only writes name and color, never ownerId. There is no ownership-transfer endpoint anywhere in the calendar router (confirmed by grepping ownerId in calendar.router.ts — all occurrences are reads only). The precondition for the TOCTOU race cannot be triggered through any tRPC mutation, making this a false positive.

dismissedFalsePositives.title: addAttendee / linkToCase check-then-create — already mitigated by DB unique constraints
dismissedFalsePositives.reason: Correctly self-assessed by auditor. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma line 1669: @@unique([appointmentId, userId]) on AppointmentAttendee; line 1685: @@unique([appointmentId, caseId]) on AppointmentCase. Both unique constraints cause Prisma P2002 errors on any concurrent duplicate insert, preventing silent duplication.

dismissedFalsePositives.title: Concurrent MFA challenge verification allows attempt-counter bypass on module-level Map
dismissedFalsePositives.reason: False positive. The entire critical section of verifyChallenge (mfa.service.ts:492-518) — get from challengeStore, check expiry, check attempts, increment attempts (line 511), call validateChallengeCode (line 514), delete on success (line 517) — contains ZERO await expressions. validateChallengeCode (mfa.service.ts:451) is a synchronous private method returning boolean; verifyTotp (line 284) also returns boolean. Because Node.js is single-threaded and there is no await in the hot path, two concurrent HTTP requests cannot interleave between the attempts++ and the isValid check. Each invocation runs the entire critical section to completion in one event-loop turn before the next begins. The 'attempt counter bypass' (N concurrent wrong codes each see attempts=0) and 'double-success' (two correct codes both return { success: true }) races described by the auditor are impossible in a single-process Node.js deployment. Cross-process deployments are irrelevant because challengeStore is process-local; a challenge created in process A is not visible to process B.
dismissedFalsePositives.mitigationFound: Node.js single-threaded synchronous execution: no await between challengeStore.get() at mfa.service.ts:492 and challengeStore.delete() at mfa.service.ts:517 guarantees atomicity.

dismissedFalsePositives.title: Query-budget store reported flag: check-then-set spans an await boundary — over-budget can be reported twice
dismissedFalsePositives.reason: False positive. packages/db/src/query-budget/extension.ts:99-101 shows: if (store.count > store.budget && !store.reported) { store.reported = true; reportOverBudget(...) }. The assignment store.reported = true at line 100 and the guard check at line 99 are SYNCHRONOUS — there is no await between the if-condition evaluation and the flag set. Node.js event loop is single-threaded; microtask callbacks run one at a time. There is no code path where two continuations can simultaneously observe !store.reported === true before either sets it to true. AsyncLocalStorage (packages/db/src/query-budget/context.ts:78) does not propagate across worker_threads boundaries, so the hypothetical multi-threaded scenario described does not apply. The finding's own notes acknowledge Node.js single-threadedness makes the risk 'theoretical'. No real concurrency hazard exists.
dismissedFalsePositives.mitigationFound: store.reported = true set synchronously before reportOverBudget call at packages/db/src/query-budget/extension.ts:100, with no await between guard check and flag assignment; AsyncLocalStorage boundary at packages/db/src/query-budget/context.ts:78 isolates stores per async context
